1:
The chapter opens on the crown of Solomon's whole enterprise, and the witnesses set the scene each in his own key. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry supplies the governing image: the temple, however richly beautified, was "like a body without a soul" while it stood empty, and "all the cost and pains bestowed on this stately structure are lost if God do not accept them." When the work was ended, therefore, "the one thing needful is yet behind, and that is the bringing in of the ark." He notes that Solomon presides in person, as David had presided when the ark came up to Jerusalem, and neither king thought it beneath him to follow the ark and lead the people; "as every man has received the gift, so let him minister."

The grammatical-historical commentators lay out the architecture of the day. Keil and Delitzsch observe that the solemn transaction "consisted of three parts, and the chapter arranges itself in three sections accordingly": the conveyance of the ark and tabernacle into the temple (vv.1–21), the dedicatory prayer (vv.22–53), and the blessing and sacrifice (vv.54–66). Charles Ellicott weighs the summons carefully: if "the elders of Israel" are to be distinguished from "the heads of the tribes," the former are the chiefs of official rank, the latter the feudal chiefs of the great families; these alone were specially called, but because the dedication was blended with the Feast of Tabernacles "all the men of Israel naturally assembled at Jerusalem without special summons."

The reformed John Gill raises the antiquarian question whether so early a date already knew "such a court" as the great sanhedrim, leaving it open. Adam Clarke, of the wesleyan-arminian school, supplies the chronology: following Archbishop Usher, he holds that Solomon deferred the dedication almost a year after the building was finished because that year was the ninth jubilee, so that the seven days of dedication and the seven of Tabernacles fell together and the atonement and jubilee trumpet sounded between. The witnesses do not divide on anything of substance here; they agree that the ark is the soul of the house, and that the gathering of all Israel to bring it home is the necessary consummation of twenty years' labour, the moment when God is invited to own what men have built for his name.
2:
The single verse fixes the season, and the witnesses turn it into a small study of the sacred calendar. The reformed John Gill is careful to distinguish the two festivals that here overlap: the assembly gathered "at the feast," which he insists was "the feast of the dedication of the temple, and which was kept before" the Feast of Tabernacles, "since both lasted fourteen days, and the people were dismissed on the twenty third of the month." A vast crowd of common people came, he adds, not merely to see the ark removed but because "in a few days was the time for all the males in Israel to appear there."

The grammatical-historical commentators concentrate on the name Ethanim and on the character of the feast. Charles Ellicott notes that the month, "called after the Captivity Tisri," answered to the end of September and beginning of October, and following Thenius reads the name as "month of gifts," so called "as bringing with it the gathering in of the vintage, and of the last of the crops"; by the Chaldee Targum it was "in old times the beginning of the civil, as Abib of the ecclesiastical year." He calls the Feast of Tabernacles "of all feasts of the year the most joyful," commemorating the wilderness dwelling and thanking God "for settlement and blessing in the land," and observes that "there was also a peculiar appropriateness in thus giving it a higher consecration" by marking the ark's transfer from the movable tent to a fixed habitation. This year, he stresses, "the festival was doubled in duration, from seven to fourteen days."

Keil and Delitzsch dispute the etymology. Against Gesenius, who explains Ethanim as "month of the flowing brooks," and against Böttcher's "equinox" and Thenius's "month of gifts," they argue from the season itself: the seventh month "corresponds to the period between the new moon of October and the new moon of November, during which the rainy season commences in Palestine," so that "this month may very well have received its name from the constant flowing of the brooks." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, more briefly, simply identifies "the feast in the month Ethanim" as the Feast of Tabernacles, "celebrated in the seventh month" of the ecclesiastical year. Beneath the small quarrel over the word's derivation, the witnesses agree on the fitness of the timing: the most joyful feast of the year, at the season of ingathering, was chosen to bring the ark of God to its rest.
3:
The verse marks a change of bearers, and the witnesses fasten on the precise ritual propriety of who carried the ark. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays out the distinction with care: "to bear the ark on its journeys was properly the duty of the Levites of the family of Kohath" (Numbers 3:31; 4:5), but "to bring it out of the Holy of Holies, and to replace it therein, was the work of the priests alone." Hence, he notes, the narrative moves with "literal accuracy" — first that "the priests took up the ark," then (v.4) that priests and Levites brought up the ark and the holy things, and lastly (v.6) that "the priests brought in the ark into the oracle." He records Josephus's variant, that on this special occasion, as at the Jordan and the circuit of Jericho (Joshua 3:6–17; 6:6), the priests themselves bore the ark while the Levites carried only the vessels.

Keil and Delitzsch add the supplementary sense of the Hebrew, that the notice about priests and Levites "introduces, according to the general diffuseness of the early Hebrew style of narrative, the more precise statement" of who brought up the sacred things. Their chief concern is to identify the tent: the "tabernacle of the congregation" here is "not the tent erected for the ark of the covenant upon Zion," which was never so called and is expressly distinguished from it in 2 Chronicles 1:4, "but is the Mosaic tabernacle at Gibeon," before which Solomon had earlier sacrificed (1 Kings 3:4). That ancient tent, from which the ark had long been separated, "was probably preserved as a sacred relic" in the temple precincts.

The reformed John Gill contributes only the plain topography — the elders "came to Zion, the city of David" — content to let the fuller commentators carry the weight of the ceremonial detail. The witnesses do not contend; they combine to show a scrupulous observance of Mosaic order at the very hinge of Israel's worship, the last journey of the ark conducted with the exact reverence the Law prescribed, so that nothing in the transfer from tent to temple should trespass on the holiness of the thing carried.
4:
Here the whole apparatus of the older worship is gathered up into the new house, and the witnesses attend to the logistics and the pathos of the moment. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke raises the practical puzzle of the two tents: "it is generally agreed that there were now two tabernacles," the one at Gibeon and the one David had pitched in the city of David as a temporary home for the ark. Which was brought in "is not well known"; some think both, "in order to prevent the danger of idolatry," but Clarke himself supposes "that the tabernacle from Gibeon was brought in, and that the temporary one erected by David was demolished."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott traces the orderly procession by the Mosaic division of labour: "the Kohathites had charge on the march of the ark and the vessels, the Gershonites of the Tabernacle and its hangings, and the Merarites of the boards and pillars," and "probably each section of the priests and Levites now brought up in solemn procession the sacred things entrusted to them." What became of the disused tabernacle "we are not told; but all was probably deposited, as a sacred relic of antiquity, somewhere in the precincts of the Temple." He notes the "famous Jewish tradition" that Jeremiah "was enabled to hide by miracle the Tabernacle and the ark and the altar of incense" at the temple's destruction. Keil and Delitzsch, quoting the verse in full, likewise stress that "the priests and the Levites" together bore up these things, and that the sacrifice of v.5 was offered "before the ark" as it was set down in the court.

The reformed John Gill marks only the route — "from the city of David to the temple" — leaving the fuller narrative to the others. There is no dispute here; the voices unite to show the old and the new meeting for a moment in one court. The movable sanctuary that had wandered with Israel since the wilderness now yields up its holiness to the fixed house, and, as Henry elsewhere puts it, all the church's holy things on earth are at last "swallowed up in the perfection" of what supersedes them — a quiet closing of one long chapter of Israel's pilgrim worship.
5:
The verse pauses the procession on a scene of overwhelming sacrifice, and the witnesses, though brief here, draw out both its precedent and its lavishness. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the moment against its great forerunner in David's reign: "this inaugural sacrifice corresponded on a grand scale to the ceremonial of the day, when David brought up the ark to Zion." He recalls that "when they that bare the ark of the Lord had gone six paces, he sacrificed oxen and fatlings," even "seven bullocks and seven rams" (2 Samuel 6:13; 1 Chronicles 15:26). The offering here, he judges, was made "before the ark, either as it left Mount Zion, or on arrival in the Temple, before it passed out of sight into the oracle" — a father's single act of worship now magnified into a son's uncountable multitude.

The reformed John Gill notes only that this was done "on this solemn occasion" by "King Solomon, and all the congregation of Israel, that were assembled together," binding king and people into one worshipping body around the ark. Keil and Delitzsch fix the place and manner: the sacrifice was offered "while the ark of the covenant was carried up, no doubt when it was brought into the court of the temple, and was set down there for a time," so that this "magnificent sacrifice" was made "in front of the ark," the animals being "in innumerable multitude."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, commenting on the whole opening scene, draws the lesson of a wealthy and grateful people: "the people in Solomon's time were very rich, very easy, and very cheerful, and therefore it was fit that, on this occasion, they should consecrate not only their cheerfulness, but a part of their wealth, to God and his honour," for "public mercies call for public acknowledgments." The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the countless victims before the ark are not extravagance for its own sake but the fitting overflow of a nation's joy — the whole people, with the king at their head, spending their substance to honour the God who is coming to dwell among them.
6:
The ark now reaches its resting place, and the witnesses linger over the cherubim that overshadow it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe the moment precisely: "after this sacrificing was ended, the priests carried the ark to its place, into the back-room of the house, into the Most Holy under the wings of the cherubim." They read the following verse as the explanation of this one — "the cherubim were spreading out wings towards the place of the ark, and so covered over the ark and over its poles from above" — and infer from the shading of the poles that "the ark was probably so placed that the poles ran from north to south, and not from east to west," correcting even their own earlier diagram.

The reformed John Gill marks the fitness of the placing: the priests brought the ark "unto his place, destined for it, the like to which it had in the tabernacle," so that continuity with the older worship is preserved even in the new and greater house. The great cherubim, he notes elsewhere, spread their wings "from wall to wall" over the place where the ark stood, filling the inner sanctuary with the emblem of angelic guardianship.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers up the theological weight of the location. The ark was fixed "in the oracle of the house, whence they expected God to speak to them, even in the most holy place, which was made so by the presence of the ark, under the wings of the great cherubim which Solomon set up" (1 Kings 6:27), "signifying the special protection of angels, under which God's ordinances and the assemblies of his people are taken." This, he says, was "the place appointed for its rest after all its wanderings." The witnesses are wholly agreed: the long homeless pilgrimage of the ark, which had known Shiloh and the Philistine cart and the house of Obed-edom and David's tent, ends here in stillness beneath the outstretched wings — a settled throne at last for the God who had so long journeyed with a wandering people, and who now consents to be sought in one appointed place.
7:
This verse dwells on the sheltering wings, and the witnesses read the image both architecturally and devotionally. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the mechanics: "the cherubim spread forth their two wings over the place of the ark, and the cherubim covered the ark and the staves thereof above." Because "the outspread wings of the great cherubic figures threw a shade not only over the ark of the covenant, but also over its poles," they reason again to the orientation of the ark, the poles running north and south. Their concern is exactness — that the sacred furniture stood exactly as the account describes, the two towering figures Solomon had made in the oracle (1 Kings 6:23) enfolding the smaller wilderness cherubim wrought upon the mercy-seat itself.

The reformed John Gill supplies the same picture in plainer words: "the cherubim spread forth their two wings over the place of the ark," that is, "the most holy place where the ark stood, even from wall to wall." The two vast wings reached across the whole inner chamber, so that the ark rested wholly within their shadow. This is more than measurement; it is the visible sign of a protected and hallowed centre, the point in all Israel where heaven bent down to meet the earth.

Since the witnesses here confine themselves to the description, the verse's own words carry much of the meaning. The staves that had borne the ark through so many perilous journeys are now themselves overshadowed and at rest, and the language of covering and shadowing runs through Scripture as the very speech of divine protection — the shadow of the Almighty, the covering of the wings under which the faithful take refuge. The commentators do not moralise the point at length, but their agreement is plain: the ark that had so often been exposed, captured, and carried is now sheltered beneath wings that will not move, and the Most Holy Place becomes what its name declares, a covered and guarded dwelling where the glory of God will shortly settle, as v.10 and v.11 will tell.
8:
The drawn-out staves and the phrase "unto this day" become for the witnesses a study in the small precisions of the sacred text. The reformed John Gill corrects a rabbinic misreading: they "drew out the staves," he says, "not made them larger, as Ben Gersom" supposed, "nor did they draw them wholly out, and lay them up in the sanctuary, there being no further use for them, the ark having now a fixed place, and not to be removed"; rather "they drew them out some little way," so that their ends could be seen. The poles that had carried the ark through the wilderness and across Jordan are neither discarded nor concealed but left protruding as a witness to the completed journey.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch settle the grammar against the same rabbinic view. The verb, they insist, "cannot be rendered: they had lengthened the poles," from which Kimchi and others inferred that new and longer carrying-poles were made; the form "is used intransitively, to be long, literally, to show length," as in Exodus 20:12 and Deuteronomy 5:16. The staves were simply so long "that their heads were seen from the Holy Place before the hinder room," yet "on the outside they were not seen." The point of the notice, they add, is to record that the poles remained visible — a fixed sign directing the high priest, when he entered once a year, toward the mercy-seat.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke seizes on the closing formula for a question of date: "and there they are unto this day," he observes, "proves that the book was written before the destruction of the first temple, but how long before we cannot tell." The little phrase thus becomes a witness to the antiquity and honesty of the record, penned while the staves still stood in their place. The voices do not divide; together they show how much the historic commentators cared for the exact truth of a detail — that the ark's poles, no longer needed for travel, were kept in reverent sight as the memorial of a pilgrimage now ended and a resting-place now secured.
9:
The bare contents of the ark draw out one of the chapter's richest reflections, and the witnesses divide their labour between textual precision and moral wonder. The reformed John Gill and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch agree on the plain fact: there was "nothing in the ark save the two tables of stone which Moses put there at Horeb." Gill, following Ben Gersom, notes that Aaron's rod and the pot of manna were "by the ark, but not in it," or at most "on the sides of it"; Keil and Delitzsch explain that the remark exists "simply to show that the law, which enjoined that the ark should merely preserve the stone tables of the covenant (Exodus 25:16; 40:20), had not been departed from in the lapse of time."

Charles Ellicott presses the same point for the sake of a famous crux. "The emphasis of this," he says, "is remarkable, and seems intended to make it clear" that the pot of manna, the rod, and the copy of the Law "were not in the ark, but at the side of the ark." This, he holds, "must determine the interpretation of the well-known passage in the Epistle to the Hebrews" (Hebrews 9:4), where "no stress need be laid on the literal accuracy of the word wherein," its purpose being only a general description. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke simply refers the reader to his note on the same Hebrews text.

Ellicott then rises to the theological height of the verse. There is, he writes, "something singularly impressive in the especial hallowing of the granite tables of the Law of Righteousness, as the most sacred of all the revelations of the Nature of God; thus indissolubly binding together religion and morality." God is best known to man "not in His omnipotence, or even in His infinite wisdom," but "in His moral nature, as the very Truth and Righteousness"; the whole prophetic office was "to bring out the truth here implied, thus writing the law on the heart and on the mind" (Jeremiah 31:33), so that "the very name of the Messiah for whom they prepared is Jehovah our righteousness" (Jeremiah 23:6). The witnesses do not contend; from a note about what the ark contained they arrive together at the heart of biblical religion, that the holiest thing in Israel was the moral law of God, and that this law finds its fulfilment in the Righteous One.
10:
The cloud that fills the house draws every witness to the theme of the divine presence, and they read it through the whole sweep of Scripture. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch anchor it in precedent: "at the dedication of the tabernacle the glory of Jehovah in the cloud filled the sanctuary, so that Moses could not enter (Exodus 40:34–35); and so was it now." The cloud, "as the visible symbol of the gracious presence of God, filled the temple, as a sign that Jehovah the covenant-God had entered into it, and had chosen it as the scene of His gracious manifestation in Israel."

Charles Ellicott expands the symbol across the canon. This was "the bright Shechinah of the Divine Presence, at once cloud and fire," which had marked God's presence on Sinai (Exodus 24:15–18) and hallowed the tabernacle, and "now similarly descended on the Temple, as a sign of its acceptance with God." He traces its later history with feeling: in Ezekiel's visions "the same glory is seen, first filling the house of the Lord, and then departing from it, as polluted by manifold idolatry" (Ezekiel 10:4, 10:18); its return "is solemnly promised by Haggai (Haggai 2:7, 2:9) in distinct reference to the coming of the Messiah," and declared to be "even greater than in the magnificence of Solomon's Temple." The symbol, he says, reveals divine glory "not in the unveiled brightness of heaven, but in the glorious cloud of mystery," through which alone "the eye of man can bear to contemplate" it; out of that glory "comes the only revelation which can be distinct to man — the voice or the word of the Lord."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke marks the timing — the cloud came "after having carried the ark into the holy of holies, before any sacred service had yet commenced." Ellicott adds, from the parallel in 2 Chronicles 5:11–13, that the glory descended "as it were in answer to a solemn burst of worship" as the Levites praised the Lord, "because He is good; for His mercy endureth for ever." The witnesses stand united in awe: the God who filled the wilderness tent has filled the settled house, taking up residence in the midst of his people and answering their praise with the sign of his own indwelling glory.
11:
The priests, unable to stand for the cloud, become for the witnesses a lesson in the proper fear of God's glory. The reformed John Gill offers three possible causes for their retreat — "either through the darkness it first caused, or through the light that broke out of it, which was dazzling to them, or through the terror it struck their minds with." They could minister neither in the holy place, nor in the most holy where only the high priest might enter and that but one day a year, nor at the altar of burnt offering in the court; the whole service was arrested by the overwhelming presence.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the moment through its parallels. Keil and Delitzsch see the same event as at the tabernacle, when "Moses was not able to enter"; the cloud did not drive the priests out, for it filled the house only after they had come forth, but "they could not remain in the Holy Place to perform service." Charles Ellicott sets the scene in a whole company of biblical theophanies: "they shrank from the glory of the Lord, whom none could see and live; just as Isaiah felt undone when he beheld the glory of the Lord in the Temple" (Isaiah 6:5), "and as even the Apostles trembled, when they entered into the bright cloud which overshadowed them on the Mount of Transfiguration, and knew not what they said" (Luke 9:33–34). Yet he carefully qualifies the emotion: "it was not so much from terror of the Lord, who is a consuming fire, as simply from awe and reverence of His unspeakable glory."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke widens the reach of the cloud: "the glory of the Lord had filled the house — the cloud appears to have filled not only the holy of holies, but the whole temple, court and all, and to have become evident to the people; and by this Solomon knew that God had honoured the place with his presence." The witnesses do not divide. Together they portray the strange courtesy of the divine glory, which suspends even the appointed ministry of the priests, so that for a moment Israel can do nothing but stand back and adore — a reverent helplessness that is itself the truest worship of a God whose nearness no servant can command.
12:
Solomon's first recorded words — that the LORD "would dwell in the thick darkness" — turn the frightening cloud into a token of favour, and the witnesses gather around both the comfort and the paradox. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears the king "encouraging the priests," who had come out "much astonished at the dark cloud that overshadowed them." His remedy is to remind them "that this was a token of God's presence: so far from being a token of his displeasure that it is an indication of his favour, for he had said, I will appear in a cloud" (Leviticus 16:2). Then comes his memorable paradox: "God is light (1 John 1:5), and he dwells in light (1 Timothy 6:16), but he dwells with men in the thick darkness, makes that his pavilion, because they could not bear the dazzling brightness of his glory. Verily thou art a God that hidest thyself" (Isaiah 45:15).

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke grounds the saying in the exodus: "it was under the appearance of a cloud that God showed himself present with Israel in the wilderness" (Exodus 14:19–20), and "at the dedication of the tabernacle God manifested himself in the same way that he did here" (Exodus 40:34–35). Keil and Delitzsch, quoting Sebastian Schmidt, agree that Solomon "assuredly believed that God was in this cloud also," referring the words back to the divine utterances "concerning the manifestation of His gracious presence," not only Leviticus 16:2 but Exodus 19:9.

The one real dispute is textual, and Charles Ellicott records it. Solomon's words are "somewhat broken and abrupt," as is natural "in a moment of mingled awe and thankfulness," yet clear: he recognises the cloud as "the sign that the Divine presence is granted to the Temple." Ellicott defends "thick darkness" against Thenius, who "from a single Chaldee version suggests the correction Jerusalem," urging a closer harmony with v.16 and Psalms 132:13–14. Ellicott calls the conjecture "ingenious, but it lacks authority, both external and internal," the Hebrew, the Vulgate, and Josephus all supporting the received reading. The witnesses agree on the deeper truth: the darkness is not God's absence but the veil of his excess of light, "darkened," as Ellicott says, "by excess of light," so that the mystery which hides him is the very proof that he has come.
13:
The verse is Solomon's solemn act of conveyance — "I have surely built thee an house to dwell in, a settled place for thee to abide in for ever" — and the witnesses read it as the formal giving of the house into God's possession. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry casts it as a legal transaction between the coming God and the welcoming king. "Since God, by this cloud, came down to take possession, he does, in a few words, solemnly give him possession. Surely I come, says God. Amen, says Solomon, even so, come, Lord. The house is thy own, entirely thy own; I have surely built it for thee, and furnished it for thee; it is for ever thy own, a settled place for thee to abide in for ever; it shall never be alienated nor converted to any other use; the ark shall never be removed from it, never unsettled again." It is, he adds, "Solomon's joy that God has taken possession; and it is his desire that he would keep possession."

The reformed John Gill marks the turn of the king's attention. Having quieted the priests and people "with a few words, he addressed the Lord," and "having built an house for him, with this view, that he might dwell in it, he was now, by the above token, fully assured it would be an habitation for him." The cloud has converted hope into certainty; the builder now knows his work is accepted.

The grammatical-historical voices confirm the sense. Keil and Delitzsch quote the verse as the pivot from the sign to the surrender, joining it to the blessing that follows, and Adam Clarke, of the wesleyan-arminian school, catches the note of settled conviction: "he was now fully convinced that the thing pleased God, and that he had taken this place for his settled habitation." The witnesses do not divide. The emphatic "surely" and "for ever" are heard by all as the language of a completed dedication — a mortal king daring, in reverent joy, to say to the infinite God that the house is his and to beg him never to depart, even while, as the next verses will show, Solomon knows full well that no house can contain him.
14:
Solomon now turns from the shrine to the people, and the witnesses note both the choreography and the priestly character of the act. The reformed John Gill pictures the movement exactly: the king "was before the altar, with his face to that first, and looking towards the holy and the most holy place, filled with the cloud and glory; and now he turned himself and stood with the altar behind him, and looking to the court of the people." The turn of the body marks a turn of address — from speaking to God to speaking for God to the congregation.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott supplies the platform on which he stood. From the book of Chronicles "we are told that the king stood on a brasen scaffold three cubits high, in the midst of the court before the altar of sacrifice" (2 Chronicles 6:13), "so that he could alternately turn towards the Temple and towards the people in the outer court." The raised stand allowed the whole assembly to see and hear their king as he blessed them.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies the likely content of the blessing and its precedents. Though the words are not recorded, "we may suppose that it was such as the high priest pronounced upon the people" — "The Lord bless thee, and keep thee; the Lord make his face shine upon thee" (Numbers 6:24–26) — "for Solomon seems now to be acting the part of the high priest." Clarke sets him among two great forerunners: Moses, who "when he had seen that the people had done all the work of the tabernacle, blessed them" (Exodus 39:43), and David, who after the offerings "blessed the people in the name of the Lord" (2 Samuel 6:18). Henry adds that the people "all stood up, in token of reverence and readiness to hear and accept it." The witnesses agree without dispute: the king who has just given the house to God now stands as mediator toward the people, a royal figure exercising an almost priestly blessing, and in that double office the commentators discern a foreshadowing of the one who is both King and Priest to his people.
15:
The blessing opens, as the witnesses observe, not with the people but with the praise of God: "Blessed be the LORD God of Israel, which spake with his mouth unto David my father, and hath with his hand fulfilled it." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes this the pattern of all thanksgiving. "What we have the pleasure of God must have the praise of," he writes; Solomon "thus engaged the congregation to lift up their hearts in thanksgivings to God, which would help to still the tumult of spirit" the cloud had stirred in them. His invitation is a model of turning fear into worship: "Come, let God's awful appearances not drive us from him, but draw us to him; let us bless the Lord God of Israel." So, he notes, "Job, under a dark scene, blessed the name of the Lord."

Henry fastens especially on the pairing of word and deed. Solomon "blessed God for his promise which he spoke with his mouth to David, and for the performance, that he had now fulfilled it with his hand." From this he draws his settled principle: "we have then the best sense of God's mercies, and most grateful both to ourselves and to our God, when we run up those streams to the fountain of the covenant, and compare what God does with what he has said." The mercy is felt most deeply when traced back to the promise that guaranteed it.

The reformed John Gill hears in the opening ascription the covenant faithfulness of God, who "had afresh shown himself to be Israel's covenant God, by taking up his residence among them in the temple he had filled with his glory." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, quoting the verse, connect the "mouth" and the "hand" of God as the twin pillars of the doxology — the spoken pledge to David and its accomplishment now before their eyes. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the right response to God's manifested glory is not first petition but praise, and that the surest ground of praise is the sight of a promise kept, the word of God vindicated by the works of God so that faith may rest on both together.
16:
The verse recites God's ancient word — "I chose no city... but I chose David" — and the witnesses are chiefly occupied with a textual gap and its theological point. Both the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observe that the sentence, as it stands in Kings, seems incomplete, and both correct it from the fuller parallel in Chronicles. Clarke, following Dr. Kennicott, notes that "the thirteen Hebrew words now lost in Kings are happily preserved" in 2 Chronicles 6:5–6, though he cautiously doubts they were ever truly part of Kings and thinks their presence in Chronicles simply "helps to complete the sense." Ellicott supplies the restored reading: "Neither chose I any man to be ruler over my people, but I have chosen Jerusalem, that my name might be there, and I have chosen David to be over my people Israel."

Ellicott draws out the exact parallel the fuller text makes plain — "the parallel in the two points referred to is exact." As "there were temporary resting places for the ark, such as Gilgal, Shiloh, Kirjathjearim, and Zion," so "there were rulers raised up successively for a time, and then removed. Now there was to be one fixed place as the Sanctuary of God, and one royal house of David to continue for ever." The verse thus couples a chosen place with a chosen person, the temple with the dynasty, both fixed by the free election of God after long provisional arrangements.

The reformed John Gill adds the chronological horizon, reckoning the exodus "now about four hundred and eighty eight years ago," so that the sweep of the sentence gathers up nearly five centuries of God's dealings with his people. Keil and Delitzsch confirm that the promise "is taken freely from 2 Samuel 7:7–8," and note that Chronicles inserts "I chose Jerusalem, that my name might be there," so that "the affirmation answers more precisely to the preceding negation." The witnesses do not divide over substance; they agree that Solomon grounds the whole temple enterprise not in human initiative but in divine choice — the election of a place and of a house — and that the care they take over thirteen missing words is itself a testimony to their reverence for the exactness of the covenant record.
17:
The verse recalls that the temple began as a desire in David's heart, and the witnesses read it as a study in the relation between human intention and divine appointment. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lays out the order of causes with characteristic clarity: "God chose the person first that should rule his people — I chose David — and then put it into his heart to build a house for God's name." The great work "was not a project of his own, for the magnifying of himself; but his good father, of blessed memory, laid the first design of it, though he lived not to lay the first stone." The purpose was David's, but even the purpose was God's gift, planted in the heart he had already chosen.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the words to their source in 2 Samuel 7:2 and 7:12–13, observing that "God approves the intention of David as such," and rendering the divine reply of the next verse, "Thou didst well that it was in thy mind." The reformed John Gill fills in the inward reality: David's "mind was disposed to it, his heart was set upon it, he had taken up a resolution." The whole design lay ready in the father before a stone was laid by the son.

Since the witnesses treat this verse and the two that follow as one continuous recital, its own words invite reflection. Solomon is careful to name David's heart, not David's hands, as the origin of the temple — an origin God honoured though he withheld the execution. The commentators find in this the dignity that God grants to holy desire even when providence forbids its accomplishment, a theme they will press home at v.18. The son who now stands in the finished house openly confesses that the vision was his father's, given him by God; and in that confession Solomon disowns all self-glory, tracing the greatest building of his reign back past his own labour to a longing that God himself had kindled in the breast of David. It is a striking humility in a king at the summit of his achievement, that he should begin the account of his temple not with his own resolve but with his father's heart, and behind that heart with the God who chose the man and planted the desire. The witnesses do not divide; they read the verse as a lesson that every good work has a hidden history reaching back before the worker, and that the truest builders are those content to complete what others were given to dream.
18:
God's word to David — "thou didst well that it was in thine heart" — becomes for the witnesses the classic text on accepted intention. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the doctrine out in full: "Sincere intentions to do good shall be graciously approved and accepted of God, though Providence prevent our putting them in execution. The desire of a man is his kindness" (Proverbs 19:22). He grounds the principle in the apostle's rule that a gift is accepted "according to that a man hath, and not according to that he hath not" (2 Corinthians 8:12): "God accepted David's good will, yet would not permit him to do the good work, but reserved the honour of it for his son." The desire was crowned though the deed was denied.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm the reading from the Hebrew, glossing the divine commendation, "Thou didst well that it was in thy mind," and tracing the whole exchange to the prophecy of Nathan in 2 Samuel 7:12–13. The reformed John Gill notes simply that the word came to David "by Nathan the prophet," the appointed channel of the promise. Together the commentators fix the verse as the record of a holy purpose divinely approved.

The pastoral force of the text, which the witnesses feel keenly, lies in its comfort to every believer whose best designs are thwarted. David longed to build and was forbidden, yet heard from God the tender verdict that he had done well to long. The commentators find here the reassurance that God reads the heart and rewards its Godward inclination even where he sovereignly withholds the opportunity to act. The building of the temple would fall to Solomon; but the honour of having desired it, and of having desired it well, remained David's before God — a distinction that dignifies intention without excusing idleness, and that turns even a denied ambition into an occasion of divine approval, as the fulfilment recorded in v.19 and v.20 makes plain. The commentators are careful to keep the balance: the text does not teach that a good wish is as good as a good work, for David's wish was ready to become work the moment God permitted it, and it was only the divine prohibition, not any slackness of his own, that stayed his hand. What God commends is a heart already bent to obey, whose only frustration is that providence has assigned the deed to another. In that light the verse becomes a quiet comfort to all whose service is smaller than their longing.
19:
The short verse records the divine reservation — "thou shalt not build the house; but thy son... he shall build the house" — and the witnesses read it as the appointed transfer of a sacred honour from father to son. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry states the point plainly: God "would not permit him to do the good work, but reserved the honour of it for his son." The prohibition laid on David was no rejection of the man but a sovereign distribution of the task, the desire belonging to the father and the deed to the son who should come forth from his loins.

The reformed John Gill notes only that the refusal "is implied in the question" put earlier through Nathan, and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, quoting the verse within their treatment of the recital, connect it directly to the promise of 2 Samuel 7:12–13, where the seed that should proceed from David is named as the builder. The commentators handle the verse briefly because its sense is transparent; the weight lies in its place within Solomon's argument.

That place is worth marking. Solomon rehearses his own exclusion from the honour of originating the temple even as he stands in the temple he has built. He is careful to record that the house rises not by his own presumption but by explicit divine designation — "he shall build the house unto my name." The witnesses discern in this a deliberate humility: the king claims nothing for himself that God has not assigned him, tracing every stone back to the word spoken to his father. The building of the temple becomes an act of obedience to a promise rather than an achievement of ambition, and the son who was named before he was born to do this work does it as a servant fulfilling a charge, not as a monarch pursuing his own glory. The commentators note how the negative and the positive are held together in a single breath — "thou shalt not build" laid upon the father, "he shall build" spoken of the son — so that the same word which closed the honour to David opened it to Solomon. Neither the denial nor the grant was arbitrary; both flowed from the settled counsel of God, who apportions his work among his servants as he wills. The completed fulfilment is then confessed with thanks in v.20, where Solomon owns that not his own hand but the LORD's word has raised the house.
20:
The verse is Solomon's confession that the promise has come to pass — "the LORD hath performed his word that he spake" — and the witnesses hear in it the language of a fulfilled covenant. The reformed John Gill fixes the reference: God "hath performed his word that he spake to David, concerning his son's building the temple." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the Hebrew as the explanation of the earlier phrase "fulfilled it with his hand" from v.15: "And Jehovah has set up His word." God, they note, "had caused Solomon to take possession of the throne of David; and Solomon had built the temple and prepared a place there for the ark of the covenant," so that "the ark is thereby declared to be the kernel and star of the temple, because it was the throne of the glory of God."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the verse into a general rule of the spiritual life. Solomon here reckons his own accession and building as God's doing, not his own: "Whatever good we do, we must look upon it as the performance of God's promise to us, rather than the performance of our promises to him." From this he draws the humbling corollary, "The more we do for God the more we are indebted to him; for our sufficiency is of him, and not of ourselves." Every accomplishment is at bottom a mercy received rather than a service rendered.

The threefold claim of the verse — Solomon risen up in David's room, seated on the throne of Israel, and having built the house for the name of the LORD — is thus read by all the witnesses as a single testimony to divine faithfulness. What might have been recited as royal achievement is instead confessed as the keeping of a word. The king does not congratulate himself on his throne or his temple; he acknowledges that both were promised long before, and that his part has been merely to stand where God set him and to build what God appointed. The commentators agree that this is the very grammar of grateful faith, which measures its own doings by the promises they fulfil and returns the glory to the God who spoke first.
21:
The recital ends where it began, with the ark — "I have set there a place for the ark, wherein is the covenant of the LORD" — and the witnesses seize on the striking use of the word "covenant." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, recalling that the ark held only the two tables of stone (v.9), explains the term as a sacred metonymy: "these are called the Covenant, that is, a sign of the covenant; as our Lord calls the cup the new covenant in his blood, that is, the sign of the new covenant, for This is my body implies, This is the sign or emblem of my body." The tables are the covenant because they bear its words and seal its terms.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott builds on this a rich theology of the divine covenants. The word, he says, "illustrates strikingly the characteristics of the Divine covenants with man." Such covenants "are not, like most human covenants, undertakings of reciprocal engagements between parties regarded as independent," for "such a conception of the relation between God and man is monstrous." Rather, "God's covenants proceed simply from His will, expressed in His call"; "they begin in free grace and blessing from Him; they require simply that men should believe and accept His call, and act in obedience to that belief." He points to the very opening of the Decalogue — "I am the Lord thy God, who brought thee out of the land of Egypt" — as describing "the gift of salvation from the mercy of God," on the ground of which, "rather than of His Omnipotence as Creator," God calls for obedience. So too, he notes, Paul appeals to Christians "by the mercies of God" (Romans 12:1), the larger covenant in Christ.

The reformed John Gill marks only that the place set for the ark was "the most holy place," and Keil and Delitzsch quote the verse as the seal of the whole first section, the covenant secured in its resting-place. The witnesses do not divide. From a phrase about the ark's contents they draw the shape of biblical religion itself: that God binds himself to his people not by bargain but by grace, giving first and requiring after, so that the tables of law lodged in the ark are, before they are a demand, the tokens of a covenant of mercy freely made.
22:
With Solomon standing before the altar, hands spread toward heaven, the witnesses turn to the man, the posture, and the meaning of the prayer he is about to make. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry dwells on the dignity of the one who prays: "Solomon did not appoint one of the priests to do it, nor one of the prophets, but did it himself, in the presence of all the congregation." That a king so learned should pray so well "is a sign that he had made a good improvement of the pious education which his parents gave him"; and that he was "not shy of performing divine service before so great a congregation" shows a great man honouring God with his greatness. In all this, Henry says, "Solomon was herein a type of Christ, the great intercessor for all over whom he rules."

The posture is read as the very language of devotion. Henry notes that Solomon "kneeled down," as appears from v.54 and the parallel in 2 Chronicles 6:13, "the most proper posture for prayer" (Ephesians 3:14), for "the greatest of men must not think it below them to kneel before the Lord their Maker"; and he "spread forth his hands towards heaven," as if "to offer up the prayer from an open enlarged heart, and also to receive thence the mercy which he prayed for." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke confirms the scene from Chronicles — the "brazen scaffold, five cubits long and five broad and three high" — and shows the gesture to be universal, citing "when I lift up my hands toward thy holy oracle" (Psalms 28:2), "lift up your hands in the sanctuary" (Psalms 134:2), and "the lifting up of my hands as the evening sacrifice" (Psalms 141:2), and even the parallels in Virgil and Ovid.

The reformed John Gill and Henry together supply the theological frame. Gill notes that "the altar was typical of Christ, who is always to be in sight in prayer, and through whom all sacrifices of prayer and praise become acceptable." Henry captures the burden of the whole prayer: Solomon asks "that this temple may be deemed and taken, not only for a house of sacrifice, but a house of prayer for all people; and herein it was a type of the gospel church" (Isaiah 56:7; Matthew 21:13). The witnesses stand united: king, altar, and posture all point beyond themselves to the one Mediator, and the house is opened not chiefly as a place of slaughter but as a place of prayer.
23:
The prayer opens with adoration — "LORD God of Israel, there is no God like thee, in heaven above, or on earth beneath, who keepest covenant and mercy" — and the witnesses trace both clauses to the deep roots of Israel's devotion. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott shows the words to be "evidently suggested by more ancient utterances of devotion," found often in the Psalms (Psalms 71:19; 86:8; 89:6) and especially in David's thanksgiving after the great promise (2 Samuel 7:22), and reaching back to "the first recorded Psalm at the Red Sea" (Exodus 15:11). In them, he says, "we trace the spiritual process by which the Israelites were trained from the polytheism of their forefathers to the knowledge of the One only God" — known first "in the close personal relation of the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, to whom none is like," and at last "by the very meaning of the name" as "the One Self-existent Being, source of all other life."

Ellicott finds the second clause no less freighted. "Who keepest covenant and mercy," a phrase "familiar in prayer" (Deuteronomy 7:9; Nehemiah 1:5; Daniel 9:4), is "clearly traceable to the conclusion of the Second Commandment" (Exodus 20:6) and the revelation to Moses in the mount (Exodus 34:6–7). It is notable, he adds, not only because it describes God "showing mercy and pity," but because it "declares this manifestation of mercy to be pledged to man as a chief part of His covenant"; so in the New Testament, "to those who claim His covenant in Christ, He is faithful and just to forgive sins" (1 John 1:9).

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the confession as the very ground of confident prayer: "this acknowledgment provides the requisite confidence for offering the prayer which is sure of an answer" (Matthew 21:22; Mark 11:24; James 1:6), noting that God has "hitherto proved Himself to be the true and only God." The reformed John Gill adds that this covenant God is thereby "distinguished from all the gods of the Gentiles." The witnesses do not divide; they agree that Solomon begins where all true prayer begins — with the incomparable God whose defining glory is that he keeps both covenant and mercy toward those who walk before him with a whole heart.
24:
The prayer turns from God's nature to his proven faithfulness: "who hast kept with thy servant David my father that thou promisedst him... thou spakest also with thy mouth, and hast fulfilled it with thine hand." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears in it the crowning of a promise: "the promise was a great favour to him, his support and joy, and now performance is the crown of it." From the pairing of word and deed he draws his rule of praise: "Fresh experiences of the truth of God's promises call for enlarged praises." The believer who has watched a divine word come true is bound to a larger thanksgiving than before.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke anchor the verse in its source. Clarke refers it to the pledge "that Solomon shall build a house for the name of the Lord" (2 Samuel 7:13), now "literally fulfilled" in the completed temple. Keil and Delitzsch note how the words "spakest with thy mouth, and hast fulfilled it with thine hand" bind the utterance and the accomplishment into one testimony of covenant faithfulness, "as it is this day."

Henry presses the pastoral use of the verse for the life of prayer. The recollection that God "hitherto had been faithful to his word" is not merely backward-looking gratitude but the very warrant for the petitions that follow: "Hitherto God has helped" (2 Corinthians 1:10), and the God who has kept the first part of a promise may be trusted to keep the rest. The reformed John Gill marks the specific content — the promise "concerning a son, his successor, and the builder of the temple" — now standing accomplished before the whole assembly. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that Solomon's thanksgiving is exact and evidential: he does not praise God in general terms but points to a definite word spoken to a named man and fulfilled in a visible house, and so teaches that the strongest fuel for praise, and the firmest ground for further prayer, is the remembered faithfulness of God to what he has actually said.
25:
Emboldened by the promise kept, Solomon now pleads the promise yet to be kept: "keep with thy servant David... There shall not fail thee a man in my sight to sit on the throne of Israel; so that thy children take heed to their way." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry marks the movement of faith from experience to expectation: "The experiences we have had of God's performing his promises should encourage us to depend upon them and plead them with God; and those who expect further mercies must be thankful for former mercies." Having thanked God for the son who now fills the throne, Solomon asks him to keep the line unbroken.

Henry is careful to note that Solomon "repeats the promise, not omitting the condition — so that thy children take heed to their way; for we cannot expect God's performance of the promise but upon our performance of the condition." He points to David's own model of prayer, "Lord, do as thou hast said" (2 Samuel 7:25), and draws the lesson that "children should learn of their godly parents how to pray, and plead in prayer." The son prays as the father prayed, taking up the very words of the promise as the ground of his request.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that Solomon frames the hope "in the same conditional form in which David had uttered it" (1 Kings 2:4) and in which "the Lord had renewed the promise to Solomon during the building of the temple" (1 Kings 6:12–13). They note further that the phrasing here "indicates the divine rejection more distinctly" than the earlier form — a solemn undertone, that the throne is promised to David's house but the enjoyment of it hangs on obedience. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that Solomon holds the promise and its condition together, neither presuming on grace nor doubting it, but pleading God's word back to him while owning that the children must walk as their father walked. It is prayer at its most scriptural — grounded on the promise, honest about the terms, as the plea of v.26 goes on to press.
26:
The petition gathers into a single plea — "let thy word, I pray thee, be verified, which thou spakest unto thy servant David" — and the witnesses read it as the hinge of the whole prayer. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry states the principle it embodies: "God's promises must be both the guide of our desires and the ground of our hopes and expectations in prayer." Solomon asks for nothing that God has not first offered; his boldness is simply to hold God to his own word, desiring only what the promise has already pledged.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch resist the notion that this verse merely repeats the plea of v.25. It "forms the introduction to the prayers which follow for the hearing of all the prayer presented before the Lord in the temple." The words "contain something more than a prayer for the continual preservation of the descendants of David upon the throne"; they "refer to the whole of the promise" in 2 Samuel 7:12–16. They note a fine grammatical point — the plural "words" (in the written text) "points back to all the words in 2 Samuel 7:17" — and they discern the particular hope Solomon has in mind: "that God would not withdraw His mercy from the seed of David, even when" they should sin, the very mercy that will carry the later petitions about captivity and restoration.

The reformed John Gill glosses the request with quiet exactness: "let thy word be verified, truly made good, and punctually performed." The witnesses do not contend. They agree that this brief sentence is the pivot on which the prayer turns from the royal house to the whole nation, from the dynasty to every Israelite and even every stranger who will one day pray toward this place. Solomon asks that God's word be proved true, and in asking it he opens the door to all the petitions that follow — for the God who verifies his promise to David is the God to whom the whole people may now bring every prayer, confident that his word cannot fail.
27:
At the height of the dedication Solomon suddenly stoops in wonder — "But will God indeed dwell on the earth? behold, the heaven and heaven of heavens cannot contain thee; how much less this house that I have builded?" — and the witnesses gather around the paradox of an infinite God in a finite house. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears a double humility: "a humble admiration of God's gracious condescension" and "a humble acknowledgment of the incapacity of the house he had built to contain God." "Can we imagine," he asks in Solomon's voice, "that a Being infinitely high, and holy, and happy, will stoop so low as to dwell upon the earth and bless the worms of the earth with his presence?" And he draws the abiding rule: "When we have done the most we can for God we must acknowledge the infinite distance and disproportion between us and him."

The reformed John Gill turns the astonishment toward the gospel. The wonder that "so great and glorious a Being should ever condescend to dwell on earth" is, he suggests, a shadow of a greater condescension — "such was the amazing condescension of Christ, the Son of God, to tabernacle in human nature with men on earth," Solomon's temple being "the figure of his body, in which the Godhead dwells" (Colossians 2:9). The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch guard the logic of the passage: the verse is "an intermediate thought" by which Solomon "meets certain contracted ideas of the gracious presence of God in the temple" — though this house cannot hold God's infinite nature, he trusts that God will yet regard the prayer offered in it.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lets the immensity of God expand into the immensity of his creation. The Hebrew plurals, "the heavens, and the heavens of heavens," imply "systems, and systems of systems, each possessing its sun," extending "beyond each other in unlimited space"; light itself travels so far that from some created suns "their light has not yet reached our earth." Yet all this, he says, is but "a speck in the inconceivable immensity of space" before the God "who inhabiteth Eternity." The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the truest note of dedication is not pride in the house but astonishment at the God who deigns to fill it, and that this very disproportion — the boundless God stooping to a walled room — is the wonder that makes the temple, and every place of prayer, holy ground.
28:
Having confessed that no house can contain God, Solomon nonetheless dares to ask that God will hear: "Yet have thou respect unto the prayer of thy servant, and to his supplication, O LORD my God, to hearken unto the cry and to the prayer, which thy servant prayeth before thee to day." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the verse as a little anatomy of true prayer. It was "a humble prayer, the prayer of thy servant"; "an earnest prayer, such a prayer as is a cry"; and "a prayer made in faith, before thee, as the Lord, and my God." The plea rests not on the king's dignity but on his servanthood: "no man's dignity in the world, or titles of honour, will recommend him to God."

The word "yet," on which the whole petition turns, is felt by the witnesses to carry the weight of the preceding wonder. Having just owned that the heaven of heavens cannot contain God, Solomon does not conclude that prayer is therefore useless, but the reverse — precisely because God is so great, his condescension to hear is the more to be sought. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch join this verse to the optative of v.26, showing that the intervening confession of God's infinity does not break the prayer but deepens it: "although this temple cannot hold Thine infinite divine nature, I know that Thou wilt have respect to the prayer of Thy servant."

The reformed John Gill marks the humility of the royal suppliant, who "though a king, acknowledged himself the servant of the Lord, and esteemed it an honour to be" so, "and who was also an humble suppliant." The heaping of terms — "prayer," "supplication," "cry" — is heard by all as the earnestness of a heart that will not be content with formal words. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that Solomon models the paradox of all believing prayer: the more one grasps the greatness and self-sufficiency of God, the more urgently and humbly one asks him to stoop and hear, resting the whole plea not on rank or merit but on the covenant name, "O LORD my God."
29:
The prayer now asks that God's regard rest continually on the house — "that thine eyes may be open toward this house night and day, even toward the place of which thou hast said, My name shall be there." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch find the ground of the request in that very name: because "the name of God will be in the temple, that is, because God will manifest His gracious presence there, He will also keep His eyes open upon it, so as to hear the prayer." The clause "My name shall be there," they note, "contains within itself the ground upon which the prayer rests," implicitly drawing on the promise of 2 Samuel 7:13.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry guards the verse against superstition even as he honours its devotion. Solomon "directs them to have an eye, in their prayers, to that place where God was pleased to manifest his glory as he did not any where else on earth"; yet the temple is "not as the object of their worship — that were idolatry — but as an instituted medium of their worship, helping the weakness of their faith, and typifying the mediation of Jesus Christ, who is the true temple, to whom we must have an eye in every thing wherein we have to do with God." He recalls that even in exile "those that were at a distance looked towards Jerusalem, for the sake of the temple, even when it was in ruins" (Daniel 6:10).

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke unfolds the meaning of the name set there: "My name shall be there — I will there show forth my power and my glory by enlightening, quickening, pardoning, sanctifying, and saving all my sincere worshippers." The name is no bare title but the pledge of God's active, saving presence. The reformed John Gill adds that the eyes open toward the house are directed "to the people that pray in it," so that the promise of regard is at once toward the place and toward the worshippers who resort to it. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the temple's dignity lies wholly in the name God has lodged there, that it is a means and not an object of worship, and that in it they discern a figure of the true meeting-place between God and man — Christ himself, toward whom every prayer must look.
30:
The petition widens from the king to the whole people and reaches its refrain — "hear thou in heaven thy dwelling place: and when thou hearest, forgive." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry catches the boldness of the plea and the depth of the pardon sought. Solomon asks God to "hear it in heaven, that is indeed thy dwelling-place, of which this is but a figure; and, when thou hearest, forgive the sin that separates between them and God, even the iniquity of their holy things." The temple below is owned to be only the shadow; the true throne is above, and even the worshippers' worship needs forgiveness.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws out the mediatorial theology latent in "toward this place." Both tabernacle and temple, he says, "were types of our Lord Jesus, or of God manifested in the flesh; and he was and is the Mediator between God and man. All prayer, to be acceptable, must go to God through Him." He presses the figure to its ground: "the human nature of Christ is the temple in which dwelt all the fullness of the Godhead bodily; therefore with propriety all prayer must be offered to God through Him." So the direction of prayer toward the house becomes a parable of prayer offered through the incarnate Son.

The reformed John Gill hears the same christological note, ascribing "the acceptance of prayers with God" to Christ's "blood, righteousness, sacrifice and mediation," typified by the temple toward which Israel looked. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that Solomon himself prayed "in the court towards the temple," so that the phrase "toward this place" fits both the people and the king. The witnesses do not divide. They converge on the great word that governs the whole prayer — "forgive" — and agree that the deepest need brought to the house of God is not deliverance from outward trouble but the pardon of sin, granted from the true dwelling-place in heaven and, as they read it, through the one Mediator the temple foreshadowed. Henry notes that this single word will recur through every case Solomon is about to name, so that "forgive" becomes the refrain of the whole intercession; for, as he elsewhere observes, since all misery springs from sin, the forgiveness of sin prepares the way for the removal of every evil and the receiving of every good. The witnesses thus hear in this verse the true keynote of the dedication — a house opened above all for the confession of guilt and the receiving of mercy.
31:
Solomon now begins the series of particular cases, and the witnesses treat this first as the appeal to God as judge of hidden truth: "If any man trespass against his neighbour, and an oath be laid upon him to cause him to swear, and the oath come before thine altar in this house." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke frames the whole sequence: "Solomon puts here seven cases, in all of which the mercy and intervention of God would be indispensably requisite; and he earnestly bespeaks that mercy and intervention on condition that the people pray towards that holy place." This first case, he says, "is one of doubtfulness; where a man has sustained an injury, and charges it on a suspected person, though not able to bring direct evidence," the accused is "permitted to come before the altar of God, and purge himself by his personal oath." Solomon "prays that God may not permit a false oath to be taken, but that he will discover the truth, so that the wicked shall be condemned, and the righteous justified."

The reformed John Gill fills in a representative wrong — a man "being unfaithful in a trust committed to him, or the like" — the kind of secret injury that no court can prove and only God can judge. Where human evidence fails, the oath sworn at the altar transfers the case to the tribunal of heaven.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the case into its enduring principle. Here Solomon prays "that, in difficult matters, this throne of grace might be a throne of judgment, from which God would right the injured that believingly appealed to it, and punish the injurious that presumptuously appealed to it." He notes the later abuse the practice bred — "it was usual to swear by the temple and altar" (Matthew 23:16, 23:18) — "which corruption perhaps took its rise from this supposition of an oath taken at or near them, for the greater solemnity." The witnesses do not divide. They agree that Solomon commits to God the cases that outrun human justice, asking that the sanctuary become the place where secret guilt is exposed and innocence vindicated, and that the God who searches the heart make his altar a seat of righteous judgment as well as of mercy, as the answering petition of v.32 requests.
32:
This verse supplies the answer sought in the previous case — "then hear thou in heaven, and do, and judge thy servants, condemning the wicked... and justifying the righteous" — and the witnesses read it as the appeal to God's discriminating justice. The reformed John Gill marks the occasion simply: God is to hear "when the injured person makes supplication to have justice done him." The prayer asks not for indiscriminate mercy but for exact judgment — that the guilty be brought to bear their own way upon their head, and the innocent be given according to their righteousness.

Because the witnesses treat this verse as the completion of the oath-case begun in v.31, its own terms carry the weight. The petition uses three verbs of God — "hear," "do," and "judge" — and the commentators feel the force of the progression: God is asked not merely to listen but to act, and to act as a judge who distinguishes. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, having called the first case one "of doubtfulness," reads the whole of it as Solomon's prayer "that God may not permit a false oath to be taken, but that he will discover the truth, so that the wicked shall be condemned, and the righteous justified" — a request answered precisely in the words of this verse.

The theological interest of the petition lies in its confidence that God will judge righteously where human courts cannot. To "bring his way upon his head" is to let the false swearer's own guilt recoil upon him, and to "give him according to his righteousness" is to vindicate the wronged. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already found in this the making of "the throne of grace a throne of judgment." The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the God invoked at the altar is a God of discriminating justice, who does not confound the guilty and the innocent but renders to each his due; and they see in this early appeal a testimony that mercy and righteousness are not opposed in God, but meet in the very sanctuary where sinners come to be forgiven and the wronged come to be righted.
33:
The second case turns from private wrong to national defeat: "When thy people Israel be smitten down before the enemy, because they have sinned against thee, and shall turn again to thee, and confess thy name, and pray, and make supplication unto thee in this house." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lays out its logic: "when their enemies make inroads upon them, and defeat them in battle, and lead them into captivity, because God, being displeased with their transgressions, has delivered them up; then if they shall turn again, confess the name of God — which they had in effect denied, by either neglecting his worship, or becoming idolatrous — and pray and make supplication," Solomon asks that God would "hear in heaven, and bring them again unto the land."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch anchor the case in the covenant threatenings, noting that it "refers to the threats in Leviticus 26:17 and Deuteronomy 28:25, where the nation is threatened" with being smitten before its enemies. Solomon is not imagining a remote possibility but reciting the very curse the Law had foretold for apostasy, and asking that the door of return be kept open when that curse should fall.

The reformed John Gill marks the realism of the supposition: such defeat "had been their case, and might be again, and was, though now a time of peace." Even at the summit of Solomon's prosperity, the prayer looks soberly forward to days of chastisement. The heart of the petition, all the witnesses see, is the appointed sequence: sin brings defeat, defeat brings repentance, and repentance — a turning again, a confessing of the name, a praying toward the house — brings restoration. The temple is thus established as the place to which a beaten and penitent people may look, not that its walls have power, but that God has pledged to hear there. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that Solomon here charts the way home for a nation that has sinned itself into ruin, and that the way runs through honest confession offered toward the house where God has set his name, as the answer of v.34 promises.
34:
This verse answers the case of national defeat with the double gift of pardon and homecoming: "then hear thou in heaven, and forgive the sin of thy people Israel, and bring them again unto the land which thou gavest unto their fathers." The reformed John Gill notes the corporate character of the transgression in view — it is "not personal, but public sins, which would be the cause of such a calamity." A nation smitten before its enemies is a nation under discipline, and the sin to be forgiven is the sin of the whole people that brought the stroke.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch keep the case tethered to the covenant. This third petition, like the second, moves within the framework of Leviticus and Deuteronomy, where the loss of the land is the appointed penalty of apostasy; and the prayer for restoration answers the promise, threaded through those same chapters, that God will not utterly cast off a people who turn back to him. Forgiveness and return are held together: the pardon of the sin is proved by the reversal of the punishment, the bringing again to the land God gave the fathers.

Because the witnesses treat this verse as the resolution of the case opened in v.33, its own structure carries the theology. The order is exact — first "forgive," then "bring again" — so that restoration follows pardon and is its visible seal. Solomon does not ask that a beaten people be spared the consequences of their sin without repentance, nor that they be forgiven without being restored; he asks that God, hearing their confession, would both remit the guilt and undo the loss. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the God of the covenant is a God who chastens without casting away, whose severity is medicinal and whose mercy is the last word, and that the land itself becomes a parable of grace — forfeited by sin, regained by forgiveness. In this the commentators find the pattern of all divine discipline, which wounds only to heal and scatters only that it may gather home again.
35:
The third case turns from the sword to the sky: "When heaven is shut up, and there is no rain, because they have sinned against thee; if they pray toward this place, and confess thy name, and turn from their sin, when thou afflictest them." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke names it plainly as the case of drought: "when, because of their sin, God refuses to send the early and latter rain, so that the appointed weeks of harvest come in vain, as there is no crop." The remedy is the same threefold turning — prayer, confession, and repentance — offered toward the house where God has set his name.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch anchor the plague again in the covenant threatenings, "when the heaven is shut up, according to Leviticus 26:19, Deuteronomy 11:17, and Deuteronomy 28:23." They fasten on the two motive-clauses of the Hebrew, reading them as "because Thou humblest them" and "because Thou teachest them the good way," and finding in the second the very reason mercy may be asked: "because God teaches His people and seeks by means of chastisements to bring them back to the good way, He must forgive when they recognise the punishment as a divine chastisement and come to Him with penitential prayer." The drought is not blind misfortune but God's own schooling.

The reformed John Gill supplies the vivid picture of a shut heaven — "when the air is quite serene, and not a cloud in it" — the cloudless sky that in Palestine spelled famine. The witnesses feel the force of the phrase "when thou afflictest them": the affliction is God's, deliberately sent, and its very purpose is corrective. They do not divide. They agree that Solomon reads the withholding of rain as a divine discipline aimed at repentance, and that the fitting response is not resentment or despair but a turning from sin toward the place of prayer. In this the commentators discern the tenderness hidden in judgment — that God shuts the heaven not to destroy his people but to teach them the good way in which they should walk, and stands ready to open it again the moment they return, as v.36 asks.
36:
The petition completes the case of drought and adds a gracious purpose: "then hear thou in heaven, and forgive the sin of thy servants... that thou teach them the good way wherein they should walk, and give rain upon thy land." The reformed John Gill reads the answer as the lifting of the stroke, God forgiving "by removing the judgment of drought upon them." Pardon and rain come together; the reopening of the heaven is the token of forgiveness granted.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch dwell on the middle clause, "that thou teach them the good way." The chastisement of drought was itself a lesson, and the prayer asks that the lesson be completed — that God would not only relieve the affliction but "teach them the good way," bringing them back to obedience. Thus the whole episode is framed as instruction: God withholds rain to humble, and gives rain again to a people he has taught, so that the discipline achieves its end in a corrected walk.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying this whole group of petitions, draws out the same combination of teaching and deliverance. He notes that Solomon asks God to "teach them the good way wherein they should walk, by his Spirit, with his word and prophets," so that the afflicted "might be both profited by their trouble — for blessed is the man whom God chastens and teaches — and prepared for deliverance." The removal of the judgment comes "in love when it finds us brought back to the good way of God and duty." The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the rain given at the last is not merely a natural relief but a covenant mercy, granted to a people whom God has both humbled and instructed; and they find in the sequence — sin, drought, prayer, teaching, rain — a small model of the whole disciplinary economy of grace, in which God's corrections are never ends in themselves but means to bring his people back to the good way and then to bless the land they walk in. The commentators note too that the rain is called down upon "thy land" — the land is God's own, given to Israel only in trust — so that the restoration of fruitfulness is at once a mercy to the people and a vindication of the Giver. Where the earlier verse had traced the plague of drought to sin, this verse traces its removal to pardon and instruction, closing the case with the double gift of forgiveness and fruitful seasons.
37:
The fourth case sweeps together every calamity that can strike a land: "If there be in the land famine, if there be pestilence, blasting, mildew, locust, or if there be caterpiller; if their enemy besiege them... whatsoever plague, whatsoever sickness there be." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke itemises the list with a naturalist's care, counting nine distinct evils — famine, pestilence, blasting that leaves "instead of wholesome grain a black offensive dust," mildew that corrodes the stalk, the locust that "covers the face of the earth for many miles square, destroying every green thing," the caterpillar or young locust bred in the land, the besieging enemy, "any other kind of plague," and sickness that impairs the body. All such cases, he says, "were to be brought before the Lord," but only by those who felt "the deep-rooted moral corruption of their nature."
