The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace each item to the covenant curses of Leviticus 26:16 and Deuteronomy 28:22 and following, showing that Solomon is again reciting the very penalties the Law had threatened against the disobedient. The "devourer" and the "locust," they note, are joined "to depict the plague of locusts more vividly," and the whole catalogue is drawn deliberately from Moses, so that the prayer covers exhaustively the disasters the covenant had foreseen.

The reformed John Gill grounds the case in memory, recalling that there had been "a three years' famine in the time of David, and it is supposed it might be again, though Canaan was a land flowing with milk and honey." Even the promised land, for all its fruitfulness, lay open to these strokes. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that Solomon means to leave no misery outside the reach of prayer, gathering public and personal, agricultural and bodily, into one vast provision, so that no plague can befall Israel for which the house of God is not a place of appeal. And with Clarke they insist that the outward calamities are meant to drive the sufferer inward, to the knowledge of the plague of his own heart which v.38 will name. The commentators are struck, too, by the open-endedness of the list — "whatsoever plague, whatsoever sickness there be" — which refuses to shut the door on any grief the future might invent. Solomon does not merely enumerate the known disasters of an agricultural people; he adds a clause wide enough to hold whatever else may come, so that no Israelite in any age could say his particular affliction was unprovided for. In this deliberate comprehensiveness the witnesses find the generosity of the whole prayer, which would leave no misery outside the mercy of God.
38:
The prayer now reaches the inmost point — "what prayer and supplication soever be made by any man, or by all thy people Israel, which shall know every man the plague of his own heart, and spread forth his hands toward this house." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry seizes on the phrase "the plague of his own heart" as the key to the whole. The sufferer, he says, will "know every man the plague of his own heart, what it is that pains him, and, as we say, where the shoe pinches." But Henry presses beneath the outward trouble to the inward: "Sin is the plague of our own heart; our indwelling corruptions are our spiritual diseases. Every Israelite indeed endeavours to know these, that he may mortify them and watch against the risings of them. These he complains of; this is the burden he groans under: O wretched man that I am! These drive him to his knees, drive him to the sanctuary."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note the sweep of the petition — "by any man, or by all thy people Israel" — so that it embraces both the individual sufferer and the whole nation, each spreading forth his hands toward the house. The prayer is deliberately unlimited in its persons even as it is inward in its object: whoever feels his own particular grief, and knows it for what it is, may bring it here.

The reformed John Gill glosses the opening clause simply, "on account of any of the above things, or any other," keeping the door open to every conceivable distress. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the deepest afflictions are those a man carries in secret, unknown to any other, and that the temple is provided precisely for these — the private plague of the heart that cannot be spoken to men but can be spread before God. Henry's reading gives the verse its enduring power: that outward calamities are meant to bring a man to know his own inward corruption, and that the truest prayer offered toward the house is the confession of a heart that has felt its own disease and turned to God for the cure, which v.39 commits to the God who alone knows all hearts.
39:
The petition rises to God's throne and rests everything on his knowledge of the heart: "then hear thou in heaven thy dwelling place, and forgive, and do, and give to every man according to his ways, whose heart thou knowest; for thou, even thou only, knowest the hearts of all the children of men." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry unfolds the verse as an appeal to three divine perfections at once. To God's omniscience: "Thou, even thou only, knowest the hearts of all the children of men — not only the plagues of their hearts, their several wants and burdens, but the desire and intent of the heart, the sincerity or hypocrisy of it. Thou knowest which prayer comes from the heart, and which from the lips only." To God's justice: "Give to every man according to his ways." And to God's mercy: "Hear, and forgive, and do."

Henry draws out the pastoral fruit of this knowledge in the closing purpose, "that they may fear thee all their days": "This use we should make of the mercy of God to us in hearing our prayers and forgiving our sins — we should thereby be engaged to fear him while we live. Fear the Lord and his goodness. There is forgiveness with him, that he may be feared" (Psalms 130:4). Pardon does not breed presumption but reverence.

The reformed John Gill notes the striking phrase "in heaven thy dwellingplace," "which was more properly so than this Solomon had built, and the Lord had taken possession of." The earthly house is real but derivative; the true throne of hearing is above. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the whole weight of the petition falls on the truth that God alone reads the heart, so that he can render to every man according to his ways with an exactness no human judge could reach, distinguishing the sincere from the hollow prayer. And they agree with Henry that this searching knowledge, far from terrifying the penitent, is his comfort — for the God who knows the plague of the heart is the God who can forgive it, and whose forgiveness begets a lifelong fear that is love, as v.40 intends.
40:
The short verse states the aim of all the foregoing petitions — "that they may fear thee all the days that they live in the land which thou gavest unto our fathers" — and the witnesses read it as the moral end toward which mercy is directed. The reformed John Gill sets the whole clause in its motive: God is to hear and forgive "for his goodness sake in hearing their prayer, removing their affliction, and bestowing his blessings on them, particularly in forgiving their sins," and the fruit of that goodness is a people bound to fear him. Mercy received is meant to produce reverence returned.

Because the witnesses treat this verse as the goal of the petition begun in v.38 and answered in v.39, its own logic carries the meaning. The fear it seeks is not the dread that drives away but the filial awe that keeps close; and it is to last "all the days that they live," a lifelong disposition rather than a passing fright under judgment. Matthew Henry had already drawn the connection at the previous verse — that the experience of answered prayer and forgiven sin "should engage us to fear him while we live" — and this verse makes that engagement explicit as Solomon's very purpose.

The pairing of the fear of God with the gift of the land is felt by the commentators to be no accident. The people are to fear God "in the land which thou gavest unto our fathers," so that the enjoyment of the inheritance and the reverence of the Giver are bound together; the land is held rightly only by a people who worship. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that Solomon's prayer never rests in mere relief from trouble but always presses on to a changed heart, and that the true answer to prayer is not simply the removal of the burden but the deepening of the fear of God. In this they find the whole disciplinary and merciful economy of the covenant summed up: God hears, forgives, and blesses, that his people may fear him and live before him in the land he gave, a fear that is itself the beginning of wisdom and the safeguard of every other gift.
41:
The fifth case turns outward to the Gentile: "Moreover concerning a stranger, that is not of thy people Israel, but cometh out of a far country for thy name's sake." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke names it exactly: this case "relates to heathens coming from other countries with the design to become proselytes to the true religion; that they might be received, blessed, and protected as the true Israelites, that the name of Jehovah might be known over the face of the earth." The temple is opened, at the very hour of its dedication, to those outside the covenant nation.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that Solomon "assumes as certain that foreigners will come and worship before Jehovah in His temple," and note the precedent in the Law itself, for "even Moses had allowed the foreigners living among the Israelites to offer sacrifice at the temple" (Numbers 15:14). The great name and the outstretched arm of the Lord, they add, had already manifested themselves in deeds that would draw the distant nations. The stranger comes "for thy name's sake" — drawn not by kinship but by the fame of what God has done.

The reformed John Gill describes the stranger sympathetically as "one of another country, not belonging to any of the tribes of Israel, yet having some knowledge of, and disposition to, the true worship of God." The witnesses do not divide. They agree that this petition is one of the most remarkable in the prayer, for it shows the worship of Israel already reaching, in Solomon's own intention, beyond Israel — the earliest anticipation of the gathering of the nations. Matthew Henry, at the following verses, will make the point explicit, that "so far was Solomon from monopolizing the knowledge and service of God" that he prayed all peoples might fear him as Israel did. Here the door is set ajar: the house built for the covenant people is dedicated also as a house of prayer for the stranger who seeks the name of the LORD from afar, as v.43 will pray in full. The commentators note the touching phrase "for thy name's sake," which distinguishes this pilgrim from the mere curious traveller: he comes not to see the wonders of Solomon's reign but drawn by the fame of Israel's God, already half-persuaded of the folly of his native idols. That such a case should be provided for at the very dedication of the temple shows how far Solomon's vision reached beyond the borders of his own people, and how the God of Israel was, from the first, the God who meant to be sought by all the earth.
42:
The parenthesis explains why the stranger comes: "For they shall hear of thy great name, and of thy strong hand, and of thy stretched out arm; when he shall come and pray toward this house." The reformed John Gill unfolds the meaning of the great name that draws them — it is the fame of God "as the eternal, immutable, and self-existent Being; of the perfections of his nature, as displayed in his mighty works." The distant nations are moved not by argument but by report; the rumour of what God has done for Israel reaches far countries and turns hearts toward Jerusalem.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as a parenthesis inserted "in explanation" of the phrase "for thy name's sake" in v.41: "for they will hear." They note that "the strong hand and the outstretched arm are connected together as a standing expression for the wondrous manifestations of the divine omnipotence in the guidance of Israel," the very language of Deuteronomy 4:34 and 5:15. The exodus and the conquest, the great acts that made Israel, are the advertisement of God's name to the world.

Because the witnesses treat this verse as the explanatory hinge between the stranger's coming and the prayer for his hearing, its structure carries the sense. The report of God's deeds produces first knowledge, then desire, then the journey, then the prayer "toward this house" — the same posture Israel itself was taught to keep. The commentators feel the largeness of the vision: the strong hand stretched out to redeem one nation becomes the magnet for all. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that God's mighty works are never for Israel alone but are meant to publish his name among the nations, so that the stranger's pilgrimage is the natural fruit of the exodus. In this early prayer they discern the missionary impulse latent in Israel's whole vocation — that the God who bared his arm to save his people did so that the ends of the earth might hear, and come, and pray, as v.43 asks him to grant.
43:
The petition for the stranger reaches its climax and its purpose: "Hear thou in heaven thy dwelling place, and do according to all that the stranger calleth to thee for: that all people of the earth may know thy name, to fear thee, as do thy people Israel." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry marvels at the breadth of it. He notes that Solomon asks God to grant the proselyte's prayer even more freely than the Israelite's, "that he may carry away with him to his own country a good report of the God of Israel"; and he draws the great conclusion: "So far was Solomon from monopolizing the knowledge and service of God, and wishing to have them confined to Israel only — which was the envious desire of the Jews in the days of Christ and his apostles — that he prayed that all people might fear God as Israel did." He hears in it a gospel note: "Thus early, thus ancient, were the indications of favour towards the sinners of the Gentiles; as there was then one law for the native and for the stranger (Exodus 12:49), so there was one gospel for both." His summary is a prayer of his own: "Would to God that all the children of men might receive the adoption, and be made God's children. Father, thus glorify thy name."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch reach the same universal aim, grounding "that all people of the earth may know thy name to fear thee" in Deuteronomy 28:10. They correct a common misreading of the final clause: "this house is called by thy name" does not mean merely that the temple bears God's name, for "the name of the Lord is always used in the Scriptures to denote the working of God among His people or in His kingdom." The naming of God's name over the house "presupposes the working of God within it," and denotes the acknowledgment of that working.

The reformed John Gill notes simply that God is asked to hear "the prayer of the stranger." The witnesses do not divide. They agree that this petition lifts the whole dedication onto a world stage: the house is built for Israel, but its ultimate design is that all peoples of the earth should come to know and fear the true God. In Solomon's prayer for the stranger the commentators find the seed of the gospel's universal reach, and the earliest royal desire that the knowledge of the Lord should cover the earth.
44:
The sixth case sends Israel out to war: "If thy people go out to battle against their enemy, whithersoever thou shalt send them, and shall pray unto the LORD toward the city which thou hast chosen, and toward the house that I have built for thy name." The reformed John Gill supplies the just occasion of such a war — an enemy "threatening to invade them, or having trespassed on their borders, or some way or other infringed on their liberties," so that Israel has "just occasion to go to war." The soldiers pray as they march, turning toward the chosen city and the house of God's name.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark the qualifying phrase "whithersoever thou shalt send them," reading the case as one in which "Israel should be engaged in war with an enemy by the appointment of God." The war contemplated is not aggression but a campaign under divine commission; the prayer for victory presupposes that the cause is God's. They defend the authenticity of this whole group of petitions against critics who thought it a later addition, noting that it is "quite in harmony with the original plan" that cases should be anticipated in which Israelites far from the sanctuary direct their prayers toward it.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke seizes the phrase "whithersoever thou shalt send them" for a sharp qualification. This case "refers to wars undertaken by Divine appointment; for in no other wars could they expect the blessing and concurrence of the Lord." There were such wars "under the Mosaic dispensation," he says, but "there are none such under the Christian dispensation, nor can there be; for the Son of man is come, not to destroy men's lives, but to save them"; and he adds his own protest that "all other wars are diabolic" save the merely defensive. Matthew Henry draws the plain lesson for the fighting man: "Soldiers in the field must not think it enough that those who tarry at home pray for them, but must pray for themselves; praying should always go along with fighting." The witnesses do not divide over the substance: even in the extremity of battle, far from the temple, the way to God remains open through prayer directed toward his house, as v.45 asks him to hear.
45:
The verse gives the answer sought for Israel's armies: "Then hear thou in heaven their prayer and their supplication, and maintain their cause." The reformed John Gill glosses the plea in a word — the prayer is "for success" — and the maintaining of their cause is the granting of victory to a people fighting at God's command. The soldier's prayer, offered toward the distant house, is met by God's own advocacy of his people's quarrel.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch treat this verse as the completion of the sixth case begun in v.44, and set it against the seventh that follows: here Israel goes out to a war of divine appointment, whereas the next case will contemplate Israel "given up to its enemies on account of its sins." The two are deliberately paired — the righteous war in which God is asked to maintain the cause, and the just captivity in which God is asked to forgive and restore. In both, the prayer is directed toward the temple from a distance, and in both the hearing is "in heaven," the true dwelling-place.

Because the witnesses keep this verse tightly joined to its case, the phrase "maintain their cause" carries the theological weight. It is the same expression Solomon will use again of his people at large, and the commentators hear in it the confidence of a nation whose battles, rightly undertaken, are God's own. To maintain their cause is to take their side, to judge between them and their enemies, to give the victory that vindicates a righteous appeal. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that God is here invoked not as a distant spectator of Israel's wars but as the present advocate and champion of a people who pray as they fight; and that the maintaining of the cause is granted only where the cause is his — a limitation the previous verse, with Clarke, had pressed. So the case of battle closes as it opened, with the assurance that no distance from the sanctuary cuts off the praying soldier from the God who set his name in it.
46:
The seventh and gravest case opens with a sobering parenthesis: "If they sin against thee, (for there is no man that sinneth not,) and thou be angry with them, and deliver them to the enemy, so that they carry them away captives unto the land of the enemy, far or near." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke makes this verse the occasion of a long and forceful note. He first defines the case: it "must refer to some general defection from truth, some species of false worship, idolatry, or corruption," for which the penalty is captivity. Then he tackles the famous clause. As commonly rendered, "there is no man that sinneth not" makes the supposition "If they sin" idle; but "this contradiction is taken away by reference to the original," which "should be translated If they shall sin, for there is no man that may not sin — that is, there is no man impeccable, none infallible, none that is not liable to transgress."

Clarke turns this into a doctrinal battle. The text as usually read, he warns, "has been a wonderful strong hold for all who believe that there is no redemption from sin in this life," but "the text speaks no such doctrine; it only speaks of the possibility of every man sinning, and this must be true of a state of probation." He presses further, in his characteristic vein, that such a doctrine "is flatly in opposition to the design of the Gospel, for Jesus came to save his people from their sins, and to destroy the works of the devil," and even calls it "a dangerous and destructive doctrine." The witnesses feel the exegetical stakes: whether the parenthesis asserts the universal fact of sin or only its universal possibility.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch keep the case within the covenant, referring it to the threat of exile in Leviticus 26:33 and following, though they note that Solomon "does not confine his prayer to the exile of the whole nation" but "extends it to every case of transportation to an enemy's land." The reformed John Gill reads the supposition soberly, of the same people who had gone out to battle now "not observing the divine commands as they should." The witnesses do not finally divide over the case itself; whatever the precise force of the parenthesis, they agree that Solomon looks the darkest possibility full in the face — that his own nation may sin itself into captivity — and prepares even for that a way of return, as the confession of v.47 begins.
47:
The verse traces the first stirrings of return in a captive people: "yet if they shall bethink themselves in the land whither they were carried captives, and repent, and make supplication unto thee... saying, We have sinned, and have done perversely, we have committed wickedness." The reformed John Gill renders the opening phrase from the Hebrew as "return to their heart," explaining it as the moment when they "remember their sins, the cause of their captivity, and reflect upon them." Repentance begins in reflection; the exile comes to himself and traces his misery to its true source.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note the pattern of the confession itself — "we have sinned, and have done perversely, we have committed wickedness" — observing that this very threefold form "was adopted by the Jews when in captivity," and pointing to its echo in the great penitential prayer of Daniel 9:5. Solomon's imagined words became, centuries later, the actual language of a captive nation's repentance, so that the prayer of dedication anticipated the prayers of the exile.

The threefold confession is felt by the commentators to be no mere accumulation but a deepening acknowledgment: they have sinned, missing the mark; done perversely, twisting from the right way; committed wickedness, in settled rebellion. Nothing is excused or softened. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that true repentance, as Solomon frames it, is neither vague regret nor mere misery under punishment but a definite owning of guilt in its full extent, and that it begins where all return to God begins — in a people bethinking themselves, coming to their heart, and confessing without reserve that the fault is wholly their own. In this the commentators find the model of penitence for every age: the captive who stops blaming his captors and blames himself, whose first free act in a strange land is to say "we have sinned," is already on the road home, as the turning of v.48 and the hearing of v.49 will show. The commentators find it moving that Solomon, at the very hour of the temple's glory, should so exactly foresee the language of a future exile, framing in advance the words his descendants would one day need. The prayer of dedication thus becomes a provision for the darkest days the nation would ever know, laying up beforehand a form of confession for a people not yet born into their captivity, and assuring them that even in a foreign land the way of return stood open.
48:
The verse presses repentance from words to whole-hearted turning: "And so return unto thee with all their heart, and with all their soul, in the land of their enemies... and pray unto thee toward their land, which thou gavest unto their fathers, the city which thou hast chosen, and the house which I have built for thy name." The reformed John Gill fastens on the totality of the return — it is made "in the most sincere and cordial manner, with great ingenuity and uprightness," a turning of the whole inner man and not a partial or grudging change.

The witnesses feel the force of the piled phrases "with all their heart, and with all their soul." This is the language of the great commandment, and its use here means that the captive's repentance is to match in depth the love God requires; nothing less than the whole self is to be turned back. And the turning has a direction: the exile, though far off "in the land of their enemies," prays "toward their land," the city, and the house. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, treating this and the following verses together, note how deliberately Solomon multiplies the objects of that gaze — the land God gave, the city God chose, the house Solomon built — so that the captive's prayer reaches across the distance to fix on the appointed meeting-place of God and his people.

The commentators discern the pastoral wisdom of this direction. The exile cannot come to the temple, but he can turn toward it; the outward posture expresses an inward orientation, the heart still fixed on the God of the covenant and the place of his name. It is the same instinct that would later move Daniel to open his windows toward Jerusalem. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that Solomon here defines the fullness of repentance — an entire return of heart and soul — and gives it a focus, so that even in a foreign land the penitent knows which way to look. The prayer thus keeps open, for the most distant and most guilty of God's people, a road back that runs through wholehearted confession offered toward the house, which v.49 and v.50 ask God to honour.
49:
The petition answers the captive's turning with the plea for a hearing: "Then hear thou their prayer and their supplication in heaven thy dwelling place, and maintain their cause." The reformed John Gill reads the request in its fullness: God is asked to hear "for their deliverance out of captivity," and to "maintain their cause" — that is, to "plead it, and do them justice, avenge their injuries, and deliver them." The same phrase used of Israel's armies in v.45 now returns for Israel's captives; God is to take up the cause of a scattered and suffering people as truly as that of a marching one.

The witnesses mark the phrase "in heaven thy dwelling place," which recurs like a refrain through the whole prayer. However far the captives are from the temple, their prayer is not heard in the temple but in heaven, so that no distance from Jerusalem can cut them off. The earthly house is the point toward which they look; the heavenly throne is the place where they are heard. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch treat this verse within the seventh case as the pivot from repentance to restoration, the hinge on which the captive's confession turns into deliverance.

Because the verse is brief and the witnesses treat it as part of the larger movement, its own logic carries the weight. Solomon does not ask that the captives be heard because they deserve it, but because they have turned; the maintaining of their cause is grounded not in their innocence, for they are confessed sinners, but in God's covenant mercy toward a penitent people. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the God invoked here is one who pleads the cause of those who have forfeited every claim, and who hears from heaven the prayer of the guilty the moment they turn. In this they find the deep comfort of the whole seventh case: that even self-inflicted ruin, even a captivity earned by sin, cannot silence the prayer of a returning heart, for the God who dwells in heaven bends to hear it and to plead the cause of those who have none but him, as the forgiveness of v.50 makes plain.
50:
The heart of the seventh case is reached: "And forgive thy people that have sinned against thee, and all their transgressions wherein they have transgressed against thee, and give them compassion before them who carried them captive, that they may have compassion on them." The reformed John Gill reads the forgiveness as proved by the homecoming — God forgives "by returning them to their own land; by which it would appear that the Lord had forgiven their trespasses." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke notes a delicate feature of the request: Solomon "does not pray that they may be delivered out of that captivity, but that their enemies may use them well; and that they may, as formerly, be kept a separate and distinct people." The immediate mercy asked is not release but the softening of their captors' hearts.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott points to the remarkable fulfilment of this very petition in history. "This prayer was singularly fulfilled at the captivity of Judah in Babylon, though we hear of no such thing in relation to the captivity of the lost tribes of Israel in Assyria." He sees it "in the exceptional favour of Nebuchadnezzar and of the Ahasuerus of the Book of Esther to the Jews in Babylon," and "still more in the greater boon of restoration granted them by Cyrus and Darius, and the Artaxerxes of the Book of Nehemiah." These things, he says, "however they may be accounted for, are certainly unique in history."

Keil and Delitzsch render the Hebrew literally — "make them for compassion before their captors" — that is, "cause them to meet with compassion from their enemies who have carried them away." The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the deepest thing Solomon asks for a captive people is not first deliverance but forgiveness, and that the token of forgiveness is God's power over the hearts of their conquerors, turning the pitiless into the pitiful. In Ellicott's tracing of the answer through Nebuchadnezzar, Cyrus, and the Persian kings, the commentators find a striking proof that the God who moves all hearts kept this promise to the letter, giving his forgiven people compassion in the very courts of their captors, and at last a way home.
51:
Solomon now grounds his whole appeal in the covenant relation: "For they be thy people, and thine inheritance, which thou broughtest forth out of Egypt, from the midst of the furnace of iron." The reformed John Gill unfolds the double title. They are God's people, "whom the Lord had chosen above all people, to be a special people to him," and his inheritance, "his portion and possession." The plea is not the merit of Israel but the ownership of God — they are his, and he does not abandon his own.

The witnesses are struck by the phrase "the furnace of iron," a searing image of Egyptian bondage. It recalls the affliction from which God first redeemed his people, and the commentators feel its force as an argument: the God who brought Israel out of that furnace, who claimed them as his inheritance at the cost of the exodus, will not now cast off the people he so dearly purchased. The very origin of the nation in redemption becomes the ground of hope for its restoration. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, quoting the verse within the close of the prayer, read it as the foundation of the request that God's eyes be open to Israel's supplication — they may be heard because they are his.

Because the witnesses treat verses 51 through 53 as the closing plea of the whole prayer, the covenant logic is what carries the meaning. Solomon asks nothing on the score of Israel's goodness; every argument is drawn from God's prior act and choice. They are his people, his inheritance, his redeemed, brought from the iron furnace by his own hand. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the strongest plea in prayer is not our worthiness but God's ownership, and that Solomon rests the fortunes of a sinning, captive people wholly on the fact that God had made them his own long before they sinned. In the furnace of iron the commentators see both the depth of Israel's original misery and the greatness of the redemption that answered it — a redemption that pledges God to hear the prayer of his inheritance still, as v.52 asks.
52:
The verse gathers the whole prayer into one general request: "That thine eyes may be open unto the supplication of thy servant, and unto the supplication of thy people Israel, to hearken unto them in all that they call for unto thee." The reformed John Gill glosses "open" as "attentive," and reads the petition as covering both the king's own present prayer and every prayer he "should hereafter put up in this place," together with the supplications of all the people. It is a summary and a sweep, embracing king and nation, this hour and all hours to come.

The witnesses feel the largeness of the closing phrase, "in all that they call for unto thee." Having laid out seven particular cases, Solomon now casts the net wide enough to catch whatever the future may bring: no prayer of servant or people, in any distress named or unnamed, is to fall outside the open eyes of God. Matthew Henry, surveying the end of the prayer, notes that "after all these particulars, he concludes with this general request, that God would hearken to all his praying people in all that they call unto him for." The specific petitions do not exhaust the provision; they illustrate a mercy meant to be unlimited.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch treat this verse with the two around it as the foundation of the whole appeal, the request that God's regard rest permanently on his people's prayer. Because the verse is brief and summary, its own breadth carries the theology. Solomon does not narrow God to the cases he has imagined but opens the door to every future cry; the temple is dedicated not for a fixed list of troubles but for the whole unforeseeable life of a praying people. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the prayer ends where it should, in a confidence that God's eyes are open and his ear attentive to all that his servants and his people call for — a general assurance under which every later generation, and every particular grief, might shelter. In this comprehensive close the commentators find the enduring warrant of prayer: not a catalogue of permitted requests, but a God whose attention is pledged to all the supplications of his own.
53:
The prayer ends where the nation began, in the sovereign act of separation: "For thou didst separate them from among all people of the earth to be thine inheritance, as thou spakest by the hand of Moses thy servant, when thou broughtest our fathers out of Egypt, O Lord GOD." The reformed John Gill enumerates the means of that separation — God set Israel apart "by his choice of them in his own mind, by the redemption of them out of Egypt, by the peculiar laws he gave them, and by the special blessings he conferred upon them." Election, redemption, law, and blessing together made Israel a people distinct from all the earth.

The witnesses note that Solomon closes his appeal, as he had built it throughout, on the ground of God's own initiative rather than Israel's desert. The final clause, "as thou spakest by the hand of Moses," roots the whole in the ancient word of the covenant, so that the prayer of the new temple rests on the oldest promises of the exodus. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, quoting the verse as the seal of the prayer, read it as the summary ground of all that has gone before: Israel may confidently be heard because God himself, of his own will and by his servant Moses, set them apart to be his inheritance.

Because the verse is the deliberate close of the long intercession, its backward reach is the point. Solomon does not end with a flourish about the temple he has built but with a confession about the God who chose the people the temple serves. The nation's whole standing is derivative — separated, redeemed, spoken to, made an inheritance — and every clause turns on a divine verb. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the surest foundation of prayer is the electing purpose of God, and that Solomon fitly ends by pleading not Israel's worth but Israel's belonging, a people set apart by God's own hand and word. In this the commentators find the whole prayer summed up: from the incomparable God of v.23 to the separated people of this verse, the appeal has rested from first to last on what God has freely done, and so on ground that cannot fail.
54:
The long prayer ended, Solomon rises: "when Solomon had made an end of praying all this prayer and supplication unto the LORD, he arose from before the altar of the LORD, from kneeling on his knees with his hands spread up to heaven." The reformed John Gill reads the whole posture as typical, Solomon here "praying and interceding for his people before the golden altar," a figure of Christ who ever lives to intercede. The kneeling king with outstretched hands becomes an image of the great High Priest at his unceasing work.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note the important detail the verse now confirms — that the whole prayer was uttered upon his knees, the mention of his rising "from kneeling on his knees" settling the question of his posture throughout. They read the clause about the spread hands as a circumstantial description carried through the entire intercession, and mark the rising as the transition to the blessing of the congregation that follows.

Charles Ellicott pauses here to record a striking event preserved in the parallel account: "at this point occurs in 2 Chronicles 7:1–3 a striking passage, describing the kindling of the sacrifice by fire from heaven, and, apparently, a second manifestation of the cloud of glory." The prayer that began under the descending cloud is answered, in the Chronicler's fuller telling, by fire from heaven consuming the sacrifice — a divine Amen to all Solomon has asked. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the rising from the altar marks the hinge of the whole ceremony, from intercession to benediction, and that the kneeling posture Solomon kept throughout is a lasting rebuke to careless prayer, "the greatest of men," as Henry said, not thinking it below them to kneel before their Maker. In the king who prayed on his knees for a whole nation, and in the fire that answered from heaven, the commentators see both the pattern of humble intercession and its acceptance, as the God who filled the house with his glory now sets his seal upon the prayer offered in it. Gill's typological reading gives the moment its fullest weight: as Solomon rose from before the altar having pleaded for all his people, so Christ, the true King and Priest, intercedes without ceasing before the throne for those he rules. The witnesses note that Solomon does not linger prostrate but rises to turn at once from prayer to blessing, from the God he has addressed to the people he must now send home — the intercessor becoming the benefactor, the whole ceremony moving from petition to praise.
55:
Solomon rises to bless: "And he stood, and blessed all the congregation of Israel with a loud voice." The reformed John Gill pictures the turn — the king "turning himself from the altar, and his face to the people, giving them his benediction, not only as the father of his people, but as preacher in Jerusalem, closing it with a word of exhortation." The blessing is at once royal and pastoral, the act of a king and the address of a teacher.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott raises the delicate question of office. "To bless the congregation was the special duty and privilege of the priests" (Numbers 6:23–27); "but throughout the whole of this narrative the king, and the king alone, is conspicuous." He resolves the difficulty by noting the character of Solomon's words: they "are not strictly of blessing, but rather of praise and prayer to God, and exhortation to the people." The king does not usurp the priestly benediction so much as give thanks and exhort, standing before the people as their leader in worship.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry catches the fitness of the moment: "never were words more fitly spoken, nor more pertinently; never was congregation dismissed with that which was more likely to affect them and abide with them." He notes that Solomon "pronounced it standing, that he might be the better heard, and because he blessed as one having authority." Keil and Delitzsch add that the blessing rests on the great covenant promises, the praise of God grounded in the rest he had given his people according to all he had spoken by Moses. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke observes simply that "this blessing is contained in" the verses that follow. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the king who had knelt to pray now stands to bless, and that his benediction is really a leading of the people in praise and a charge to obedience — a fitting close to the dedication, in which the whole assembly is sent from the house of God with words designed to remain with them and shape their walk, as v.56 begins.
56:
The blessing opens, as the prayer had, with praise: "Blessed be the LORD, that hath given rest unto his people Israel, according to all that he promised: there hath not failed one word of all his good promise, which he promised by the hand of Moses his servant." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry marks that Solomon "stood up to bless the congregation, but began with blessing God, for in every thing we must give thanks." He notes the particular blessing singled out: God "has given, he does not say wealth, and honour, and power, and victory, to Israel, but rest, as if that were a blessing more valuable than any of those." And he seizes the great declaration of faithfulness: "There has not failed one word of all his good promises. This discharge he gives in the name of all Israel, to the everlasting honour of the divine faithfulness" — "he does, as it were, write a receipt in full on the back of these bonds."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott traces the theme of rest through the whole history. "Now for the first time the frequent promise of rest" (Exodus 33:14; Deuteronomy 12:10), "partially fulfilled after the conquest of the days of Joshua" (Joshua 21:44–45) and after David's victories (2 Samuel 7:1), "was perfectly accomplished under Solomon the Peaceful." Yet he sounds a warning note: "Solomon's subsequent words imply that entering into that rest was conditional on fulfilment of Israel's part in the covenant"; that condition "he himself broke, and all Israel with him," so that "the glory of rest and happiness of his age was but a gleam of prosperity, soon to be swallowed up in dissension and disaster."

Keil and Delitzsch ground the praise in the same promises, noting that God "had already given Israel rest by means of Joshua," but that this rest "was only fully secured by David's victories," and that the fulfilment now warranted hope for the future. The witnesses do not divide over the fact — that God had kept every word — though Ellicott alone presses the shadow that this very rest was forfeitable and would be forfeited. They agree in the confession that stands as the ground of all thanksgiving: not one word of God's good promise had failed, so that the surest object of praise is the tested and proven faithfulness of God.
57:
The blessing turns to petition: "The LORD our God be with us, as he was with our fathers: let him not leave us, nor forsake us." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry names this the first and greatest of the four things Solomon desires for his people — "the presence of God with them, which is all in all to the happiness of a church and nation." He feels the poignancy of the moment: "this great congregation was now shortly to be scattered, and it was not likely that they would ever be all together again in this world," so Solomon dismisses them with this: "The Lord be present with us, and that will be comfort enough when we are absent from each other."

The reformed John Gill dwells on the incomparable worth of the thing asked. "Nothing is more desirable than the presence of God; Solomon could not have prayed for a greater blessing for himself and his people." The plea reaches back to the fathers — "Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and those that came out of Egypt, and especially that entered into the land of Canaan under Joshua" — asking that the God who guided, protected, and prospered them be no less present now. The prayer is that the covenant God of the past would be the covenant God of the present and future, neither leaving nor forsaking.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch quote the verse as the opening of the intercession that runs through the blessing, joining it to the prayer that God would "incline our hearts unto him." The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the sum of all blessing is the abiding presence of God, and that Solomon, at the height of Israel's prosperity, asks not for the increase of wealth or dominion but for the one thing that makes any people happy — that God would be with them as with their fathers. In the twin negatives "let him not leave us, nor forsake us" the commentators hear the deepest anxiety and the deepest hope of the covenant people: that their whole security lies not in their temple or their king but in the continued presence of their God, without which the finest house is empty, and with which even a scattered people is never alone.
58:
The petition deepens from presence to grace: "That he may incline our hearts unto him, to walk in all his ways, and to keep his commandments." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry names this the second thing Solomon desires — "the power of his grace upon them." He fastens on the word "incline": "Our hearts are naturally averse to our duty, and apt to decline from God; it is his grace that inclines them, grace that must be obtained by prayer." And he prizes these blessings above all outward ones: "Spiritual blessings are the best blessings, with which we should covet earnestly to be blessed." Solomon asks not that God enlarge their borders but "that he may incline our hearts to himself."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott finds in the verse, taken with the exhortation that follows, one of Scripture's clearest statements of the mystery of grace and freedom. Here is "the faith which pervades all Holy Scripture" — "a faith in the true, though mysterious, co-operation of the preventing grace of God" with "that free responsibility of man which is the ultimate truth of the inner human consciousness. God inclines the heart, and yet the heart must yield itself." He notes how this conviction "grows deeper and plainer" as men grasp the inner life of the soul, coming out "especially in the Psalms, the Proverbs, and the Prophetic books"; and in the New Testament it reaches "all its fulness, perhaps most vividly in the celebrated paradox of Philippians 2:12–13: Work out your own salvation... for it is God which worketh in you both to will and to do of His good pleasure."

The reformed John Gill reads the inclining as the Spirit's work, that God would move the heart "by his Spirit, to love, fear, and serve him; to attend to his worship, word, and ordinances." The witnesses do not divide. They agree that obedience is God's gift before it is man's achievement, and that the very will to walk in God's ways must be given by the grace that inclines the heart. In this the commentators find the answer to the exhortation of v.61: Solomon can charge the people to be perfect with God only because he has first prayed that God would incline their hearts to be so — the command and the grace held together, as they are throughout Scripture.
59:
Solomon prays that his intercession itself may abide before God: "let these my words, wherewith I have made supplication before the LORD, be nigh unto the LORD our God day and night, that he maintain the cause of his servant, and the cause of his people Israel at all times, as the matter shall require." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears in this the third of Solomon's desires — "an answer to the prayer he had now made." He lifts it at once to its antitype: "What Solomon asks here for his prayer is still granted in the intercession of Christ, of which his supplication was a type; that powerful prevailing intercession is before the Lord our God day and night, for our great Advocate attends continually to this very thing." Christ maintains "the common cause of his people Israel, at all times," against "the adversary that accuses us day and night" (Revelation 12:10), so that from him "we shall receive grace sufficient, suitable, and seasonable, in every time of need" (Hebrews 4:16).

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the closing phrase from the Hebrew — "as every day demands," the day's matter in its day (Exodus 5:13; 16:4) — reading the whole as a prayer that Solomon's words might remain near to God continually, to secure the right of king and people at all times. The intercession is not for one occasion but for perpetual advocacy.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke notes that this verse and the next form "a sort of supplement to the prayer," and points to the fuller ending preserved in the parallel — "Now therefore arise, O Lord God, into thy resting place, thou and the ark of thy strength: let thy priests be clothed with salvation, and let thy saints rejoice in goodness" (2 Chronicles 6:41–42). The witnesses do not divide. They agree that Solomon desires his prayer to be a standing, ceaseless plea before God, and in that desire the commentators find a bright type of the never-failing intercession of Christ, whose advocacy is "before the Lord our God day and night," so that the cause of God's people is pleaded without interruption and answered as each day's need requires.
60:
The blessing lifts to its widest horizon: "That all the people of the earth may know that the LORD is God, and that there is none else." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry names this the fourth of Solomon's desires — "the glorying of God in the enlargement of his kingdom among men." He is careful to note the unselfish reach of it: "not that all people may become tributaries to us — Solomon sees his kingdom as great as he desires — but that all people may know that the Lord is God, and he only, and may come and worship him." With this, he says, "Solomon's prayers, like the prayers of his father David, the son of Jesse, are ended" (Psalms 72:19–20): "Let the whole earth be filled with his glory." And he adds, "we cannot close our prayers with a better summary than this, Father, glorify thy name."

The reformed John Gill traces the means by which the nations are to come to this knowledge — God makes himself known "by chastising the people of Israel when they sinned; by hearing and answering their prayers; by forgiving their sins, and delivering them out of their troubles; by maintaining their cause." The whole history of Israel, its discipline and its deliverances alike, is meant to publish to the world that the LORD alone is God, "all being else fictitious deities."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch quote the verse as the goal of the blessing, joining it to the exhortation that follows: because God's dealings with Israel are to convince the earth, the people themselves must have hearts perfect with him. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that Solomon's prayer, and indeed the whole existence of Israel and its temple, points beyond the nation to the conversion of the world — that the ultimate purpose of every mercy shown to Israel is that all peoples might know the true God and abandon their idols. In this universal aim the commentators find the same missionary breadth that ran through the prayer for the stranger, and the fitting close of the whole intercession: a desire not for Israel's supremacy but for God's glory over all the earth.
61:
The blessing ends in a charge: "Let your heart therefore be perfect with the LORD our God, to walk in his statutes, and to keep his commandments, as at this day." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads it as Solomon's parting admonition, in which, "having spoken to God for them, he here speaks from God to them." He unfolds the perfection required with precision: "Let your obedience be universal, without dividing — upright, without dissembling — constant, without declining; this is evangelical perfection." The demand is not sinlessness but wholeness of heart, an obedience undivided in its scope, sincere in its motive, and steady in its course.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix the sense of the key word from the Hebrew: the heart is to be "wholly, undividedly devoted to the Lord," the same expression that will later be turned against Solomon himself and his successors (1 Kings 11:4; 15:3, 15:14). A perfect heart is not a flawless one but a single one, given whole to God and not shared with idols. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke glosses it in two phrases: "Be sincere in your faith, be irreproachable in your conduct."

The witnesses feel the weight of the connection with the preceding prayer. Solomon can command a perfect heart only because he has just prayed that God would incline the heart (v.58); the charge rests on the grace. This, the commentators note, is the scriptural order — the duty is real and pressing, yet the ability to perform it is sought from God. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that Solomon's closing word to his people is a call to wholehearted, undivided devotion, the very perfection that his own later heart would tragically fail to keep; and they find in the phrase "as at this day" a summons to make the fervour of this great occasion the standard of every ordinary day. The command and the grace, the charge and the prayer, stand together, and the people are sent home not merely blessed but bound. Keil and Delitzsch's observation gives the verse a tragic depth: the very word for the perfect, undivided heart is the standard by which the later kings, and Solomon first of all, would be found wanting, so that this closing exhortation reads in hindsight almost as an unmet ideal. Yet the commentators refuse to let the failure empty the command of force; the call to an undivided heart remains binding on every hearer, and the grace prayed for at the previous verse remains available to answer it. The people leave the dedication under a charge as searching as the blessing was generous.
62:
The dedication passes from words to worship: "And the king, and all Israel with him, offered sacrifice before the LORD." The reformed John Gill fills in what the Chronicler adds — that when Solomon had ended his prayer and blessing, "fire came down from heaven, and consumed them, which showed the Lord's acceptance of the sacrifices, and was another confirmation, besides the cloud, of the Lord's well pleasedness with the temple." The prayer offered under the cloud is sealed by fire; the God who took possession now signifies his acceptance of the offerings, and the people bow and worship.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note the enormous scale of the thank-offering, "in accordance with the magnitude of the manifestation of divine grace," and take up a critic's objection. Thenius had tried to prove the numbers "too large," calculating that five oxen and twenty-five sheep must have been offered every minute for the king alone. But this reckoning, they answer, rests on three false assumptions — "that the number of sacrifices was offered for the king alone; that the slaughtering could only be performed by the priests and Levites; and that the whole of the flesh was to be consumed upon the altar" — none of which the text requires.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the whole festive close, draws the note of joy: the people "found Solomon's words true concerning Wisdom's ways, that they are ways of pleasantness. They had abundant joy and satisfaction while they attended at God's house." The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the offering of king and people together is the fitting response to God's manifested acceptance, and that the fire from heaven and the multitude of sacrifices answer the cloud that filled the house — the whole nation, with its king at its head, pouring out its wealth in worship before a God who has proved his presence by cloud and by fire. In this joyful, costly sacrifice the commentators see the proper issue of the whole dedication, a people moved from awe to adoration and from prayer to praise, as the numbers of v.63 record.
63:
The scale of the offering is now told: "Solomon offered a sacrifice of peace offerings... two and twenty thousand oxen, and an hundred and twenty thousand sheep. So the king and all the children of Israel dedicated the house of the LORD." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the number against the pagan world and the wilderness past: "the heathen thought themselves very generous when they offered sacrifices by hundreds, hecatombs they called them; but Solomon out-did them: he offered them by thousands." When Moses dedicated the altar the peace-offerings were but a few (Numbers 7:88); "then the people were poor, but now that they had increased in wealth more was expected from them. Where God sows plentifully he must reap accordingly." And he presses the searching application: "Few are thus minded, to spend more on their souls than on their bodies."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott faces the number honestly. It "can hardly be supposed due to any error in the text, for it is exactly reproduced in the Chronicles and by Josephus," and the real difficulty is not the supply of victims from so vast a multitude but how, even in fourteen days, they could all be offered. He suggests that "it might be deemed sufficient actually to sacrifice only certain representative victims of each hecatomb, and simply to dedicate the rest to the Lord." Yet he sounds a warning: this profusion "may perhaps have involved something of the idea, so frequent in heathen sacrifice and so emphatically condemned by the prophets, that the Lord would be pleased with thousands of rams and ten thousands of rivers of oil" (Micah 6:7) — "in these ideas lurked the evils which hereafter were to overthrow the prosperity of Israel."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the figure as "the whole amount of the victims offered during the fourteen days," and the reformed John Gill notes that the peace-offerings furnished the feast with which Solomon "feasted the people all the days." The witnesses do not fully divide, though Ellicott alone hears in the very lavishness a lurking danger. They agree that the immense sacrifice expresses the overflowing gratitude and wealth of the nation; but the sharper eye of Ellicott sees that magnificence in worship, if it drifts toward the heathen notion of buying God's favour, carries the seed of Israel's later ruin.
64:
The multitude of offerings overflows the appointed altar: "The same day did the king hallow the middle of the court that was before the house of the LORD: for there he offered burnt offerings... because the brasen altar that was before the LORD was too little to receive" them all. The reformed John Gill explains the act as a temporary consecration for present need — Solomon "sanctified for present use" the middle part of the court of the priests, "to offer sacrifices on, for a reason hereafter given." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states the reason plainly: "the great altar of burnt-offerings was not sufficient for the number of sacrifices which were then made; therefore the middle of the court was set apart, and an altar erected there for the same purpose."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the practical encouragement from the overflow. The brazen altar "was not large enough to receive all these sacrifices, so that, to serve the present occasion, they were forced to offer many of them in the middle of the court," whether on temporary altars of earth or stone "erected for the purpose and taken down when the solemnity was over," or on the bare ground. From this he takes a generous lesson: "Those that will be generous in serving God need not stint themselves for want of room and occasion to be so." Where the heart is enlarged, means will be found.

Because the witnesses treat this verse as a practical footnote to the great sacrifice, its own detail carries the meaning. The consecrated altar proved too small for the day's devotion, and rather than curtail the offerings, Solomon hallowed more ground. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the overflowing of the altar is a happy problem, the sign of a worship too abundant for its usual channels, and that Solomon's response — to enlarge the holy space rather than restrain the offering — is the right instinct of generous piety. In the too-little altar and the hallowed court the commentators find a small parable: that true devotion presses beyond its ordinary limits, and that God is honoured when his people, rather than measuring out a stinted service, make room for all their thankfulness to be poured out before him, as the great feast of v.65 continues.
65:
The dedication broadens into a national festival: "at that time Solomon held a feast, and all Israel with him, a great congregation, from the entering in of Hamath unto the river of Egypt, before the LORD our God, seven days and seven days, even fourteen days." The grammatical-historical commentators fix the geography that marks the full extent of the land. Charles Ellicott explains that "the entering in of Hamath is the significant name given to the great valley between Lebanon and Anti-Lebanon, which the Greeks called Cœle-Syria," the northern gateway "down which the hosts of Assyria and Babylon so constantly poured"; while "the river of Egypt is not the Nile, but the torrent" that falls into the sea at El-Arish, the southern border named in Numbers 34:5 and Joshua 15:4. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke agrees, taking the phrase to mean "from one extremity of the land to the other."

The doubling of the feast draws the commentators' notice. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry observes that Solomon "kept the feast, the feast of tabernacles, as it should seem, after the feast of dedication, and both together lasted fourteen days, yet they said not, Behold, what a weariness is this." The joy of the occasion swallowed up all sense of tedium; two weeks of worship were not too long for a grateful people. Keil and Delitzsch describe the assembly as "a great assembly from the neighbourhood of Hamath to the brook of Egypt, that is, from the whole land in its fullest extent from north to south."

The reformed John Gill notes that the people were "partaking of the parts of the peace offerings which belonged to" the king, together with whatever "a liberal prince" might provide for their entertainment. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the dedication climaxes in a feast that gathered all Israel, from border to border, into one prolonged celebration before God — the whole extent of the promised land represented, the fullest bounds ever reached, assembled in worship at the house now filled with God's glory. In the fourteen unwearied days the commentators see the proper fruit of God's manifested presence: not a solemn dread only, but an abounding, corporate joy that could not be exhausted, sending the people home at last, as v.66 tells, glad of heart.
66:
The great assembly disperses in joy: "On the eighth day he sent the people away: and they blessed the king, and went unto their tents joyful and glad of heart for all the goodness that the LORD had done for David his servant, and for Israel his people." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry watches them carry their joy home: "when they were dismissed they blessed the king, applauded him, admired him, and returned him the thanks of the congregation, and then went to their tents joyful and glad of heart." He fastens on the true source of their gladness: "God's goodness was the matter of their joy, so it should be of ours at all times." And he turns it into a pattern for worshippers: "thus should we go home rejoicing from holy ordinances, and go on our way rejoicing for God's goodness to our Lord Jesus, of whom David his servant was a type, and to all believers, his spiritual Israel."

The reformed John Gill and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch attend to the timing of the dismissal. Gill notes that the people were sent away "at the close of" the eighth day of the feast, yet "did not go until the twenty third" of the month, according to the fuller record. The witnesses read the eighth-day dismissal as the ordered close of the whole solemnity, the people released at last from the longest festival of Israel's history.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sounds both the happiness and its coming shadow. The people, he says, were "contented with their king, at peace among themselves, and happy in their God; so that they returned to their houses magnifying their God for all his bounty to them, their country, and their king." But he cannot close without the sorrowful sequel: "How happy must these people have been, and how prosperous, had their king continued to walk uprightly before God. But alas! the king fell, and the nation followed his example." The witnesses do not divide over the joy of the hour, though Clarke alone lets the eye travel forward to its loss. They agree that the chapter ends on the true note of dedication — a people going home glad of heart because of God's goodness — even as the more sombre voices remember that this gleam of glory was soon to be swallowed up, and that the rejoicing crowd walked homeward from the brightest day their nation would ever see.
